Approved amendments are reflected in the budget system by April 30 and communicated to cost center owners in writing. Q2 Budget Review — July The Q2 review is held during the first two weeks of July, covering actual results for April through June and the full first half of the year. This is the most consequential quarterly review because it informs the Q2 reforecast, which may result in material adjustments to the second-half plan. The review is a full-day session, typically five to six hours. Attendees: CFO, CEO, VP Finance, FP&A lead, all finance business partners, all department heads, and the Chief Revenue Officer. The board audit committee chair receives the Q2 review package as a read-only distribution within five business days of the session. What is reviewed: Actuals versus budget for Q2 and the full first half. Detailed variance analysis with written explanations required for all variances exceeding the materiality threshold. Updated full-year revenue forecast compared to the annual plan, including deal pipeline coverage, renewal rates, and expansion revenue. Second-half operating expense plan versus first-half run rate. Headcount plan versus actuals, including a review of open requisitions and projected hiring velocity for the remainder of the year. Cash position and updated twelve-month cash flow forecast. A formal Q2 reforecast for the full year, presented by FP&A, which the CFO and CEO must approve before it is distributed externally or used in board materials. The Q2 review session concludes with a structured decision segment where the CFO and CEO make explicit go or no-go decisions on any material investments, headcount additions, or spend holds that were flagged during the session. Decisions are documented in the Q2 review minutes, which are distributed to all attendees and retained in the Finance document archive. Approval process: The Q2 reforecast, once approved by the CFO and CEO in the session or within three business days afterward, constitutes the revised operating plan for the second half. Department heads whose second-half budgets differ materially from the original annual plan are issued amended cost center budgets by July 31. Any second-half headcount additions approved in the Q2 review are treated as plan-approved and do not require a separate CFO approval when the job description is posted. Q3 Budget Review — October The Q3 review is held during the first two weeks of October, covering actual results for July through September. Because October also marks the start of the annual planning cycle for the following year, the Q3 review serves two purposes: it closes out the current-year outlook and provides the baseline assumptions for the following year's budget guidance. Attendees: CFO, VP Finance, FP&A lead, all finance business partners, and all department heads. The CEO attends a summary segment at the end of the session focused on year-end outlook and next-year preliminary signals. Apex Systems Financial Reporting Policy Effective January 1, 2026 This policy defines the schedule, content, internal controls, and distribution standards for all financial reporting produced by Apex Systems. It applies to the Corporate Accounting team, the Financial Planning and Analysis team, the Revenue Operations team, and the Tax and Regulatory team. It also applies to any functional leader or department head who reviews, approves, or relies on financial data for decision-making. Accounting Framework and Standards Apex Systems prepares its consolidated financial statements in accordance with United States Generally Accepted Accounting Principles as issued by the Financial Accounting Standards Board. The company is a private entity and is not currently required to file periodic reports with the Securities and Exchange Commission. However, the company operates as if it were subject to SEC reporting standards in preparation for a future initial public offering or significant financing transaction. This includes maintaining internal controls, audit-committee oversight, and disclosure standards consistent with a registered reporting company. The independent external auditor is engaged annually through a competitive RFP process. The audit committee of the board of directors, which consists of at least two independent directors with financial expertise, selects the auditor and approves the annual engagement letter. The external auditor issues an audit opinion on the annual consolidated financial statements; a review opinion is issued for each quarter-end balance sheet and income statement, except the fourth quarter, which is covered by the annual audit. Financial statements include the consolidated balance sheet, the consolidated statement of operations, the consolidated statement of cash flows, the consolidated statement of changes in stockholders' equity, and the notes to the financial statements. The notes include, at minimum: summary of significant accounting policies, revenue disaggregation, stock-based compensation, lease obligations, commitments and contingencies, related-party transactions, and segment information. Monthly Accounting Close The monthly close cycle is designed to produce accurate, reviewed financial statements within five business days of the last calendar day of the month. Each step in the close process is tracked in the close management system with an owner and a due time. Business day 1: The accounting team reconciles all bank accounts, clears outstanding checks and deposits, and verifies that all cash transactions are recorded. The payroll team confirms that the most recent payroll is fully posted and reconciled to the general ledger. Business day 2: Accounts payable is closed for the prior month. Accruals are created for expenses incurred but not yet invoiced, based on contracts, purchase orders, and manager estimates. The most significant accruals — engineering infrastructure, marketing programs, and professional services — are reviewed by the finance business partner before posting. Business day 3: Revenue operations closes the revenue subledger. Deferred revenue schedules are updated for all new bookings, renewals, and cancellations. Usage-based revenue is calculated from metering data and reconciled to the billing system. Any discrepancies between the billing system and the revenue subledger exceeding $10,000 are flagged and investigated before the revenue numbers are finalized. Business day 4: Intercompany eliminations are applied for any transactions between Apex entities. Foreign currency remeasurement is applied to any balance sheet

Annual subscriptions are typically billed upfront, creating deferred revenue that is recognized ratably over 12 months. Professional services revenue represents implementation, configuration, and training services contracted separately from the platform subscription. Revenue is recognized as services are delivered using an input-based measure of progress, measured by hours incurred relative to total estimated hours. Significant deviations between estimated and actual hours are investigated by the project manager and revenue operations to determine whether the estimate should be updated. Usage-based revenue above a committed minimum is recognized in the period consumed. Metering data is extracted from the platform at month end, validated against the billing system, and recorded in the revenue subledger before close. Usage that exceeds the committed contractual minimum triggers an overage invoice within 15 days of month end. Contract modifications are evaluated to determine whether they constitute a termination of the existing contract combined with a new contract, a prospective change, or a cumulative catch-up adjustment. The Revenue Operations team consults with Finance on each modification that changes the total transaction price by more than $50,000. Deferred Revenue and Billings The deferred revenue balance represents amounts invoiced to customers for services not yet delivered. Deferred revenue is the single largest liability on the Apex balance sheet and is reviewed in detail at each quarter end by the external auditors. The deferred revenue subledger tracks each contract individually, with the original contract start date, the contract end date, the total transaction price, the cumulative amount recognized, and the remaining deferred balance. The subledger is reconciled to the general ledger daily; any reconciling item is investigated and resolved before the next business day. Billings — the total value of invoices issued in a period — is a key operating metric tracked separately from recognized revenue. Billings growth reflects new sales momentum and renewal health. The difference between billings and revenue in any period represents the change in deferred revenue. FP&A monitors the billings-to-revenue ratio monthly as a leading indicator of future revenue growth. Key Performance Indicators and Metrics Apex tracks both GAAP financial metrics and non-GAAP operating metrics. All metric definitions are documented in the metrics dictionary maintained by FP&A. Changes to any definition require CFO approval and must be disclosed in reporting materials with a note identifying the period in which the change took effect and the impact of the change on prior periods. GAAP metrics: total revenue, gross profit, gross margin percentage, operating expense by natural account, operating income (loss), net income (loss), and cash and cash equivalents. Non-GAAP operating metrics: annual recurring revenue (ARR), defined as the annualized value of all active subscription contracts as of the measurement date, excluding one-time fees and services. Net new ARR, the change in ARR from one period to the next. Gross dollar retention (GDR), the percentage of beginning ARR retained from existing customers excluding expansions, measured annually. SDKs and Client Libraries Official SDKs are maintained by Apex and published under the Apache-2.0 license. Available for Python 3.10 and later, TypeScript 5.0 and later, Java 17 LTS and later, and Go 1.22 and later. Each SDK provides typed models for all request and response objects, automatic retry logic with exponential backoff, transparent token refresh, rate-limit handling, and streaming support for SSE query results. SDK releases are versioned independently from the API. Each SDK documents which API versions it supports. Customers using a supported SDK are notified of API deprecations through the SDK's release notes. Community-maintained SDKs for Ruby and .NET are linked from the developer portal but are not supported by Apex. SDK installation examples: Python: pip install apex-sdk. TypeScript: npm install @apex-systems/sdk. Go: go get github.com/apex-systems/apex-go. Support and SLA API support is available through the in-product support widget, the customer portal, and a dedicated Slack Connect channel for Enterprise customers. Response time SLAs by severity: P1 (service unavailable) — 15 minutes to engagement, 4 hours to resolution target. P2 (degraded service) — 1 hour to engagement. P3 (questions or feature requests) — 1 business day. The API health status, including current incidents and historical uptime, is published at status.apex-systems.example. Customers should subscribe to status page updates to receive notifications when incidents are declared and resolved.

Apex Systems Engineering Security Guidelines Effective January 1, 2026 These guidelines define the security practices required of every engineer at Apex Systems. Compliance is mandatory. Security is not the responsibility of the Security team alone — every engineer is expected to understand the risks relevant to their work, apply secure practices consistently, and report concerns promptly. The guidelines are reviewed and updated annually by the Security Engineering team in collaboration with the General Counsel and Chief Technology Officer. Access Control and Least Privilege All access to production systems, cloud infrastructure, internal tools, and sensitive data must follow the principle of least privilege. Employees and service accounts are granted only the access necessary to perform their defined job functions. Access is not granted speculatively or "just in case." Access to production environments requires approval from the employee's engineering manager. Access requests are submitted through the Identity and Access Management system and reviewed within two business days. Approvals are time-limited: production access grants expire after ninety days and must be renewed. Access for contractors and temporary workers expires automatically at the end of the engagement. Shared credentials are prohibited. Each engineer must have a unique account for every system they access. Service accounts used by automated systems must be distinct from human user accounts and must follow the naming convention defined in the infrastructure runbook. Service account credentials must be stored in the secrets manager, not in code repositories, configuration files, or wikis. Privileged access — including root access, database administrative access, cloud account root credentials, and access to security tooling — requires a second approval from the Security Engineering team and is audited monthly. Privileged sessions must be conducted through a bastion host or privileged access workstation. All privileged session activity is logged. Access is revoked immediately upon employee departure, role change, or project reassignment that no longer requires it. People Operations notifies the Identity and Access Management team on the day of an employee departure. The IAM team completes access revocation within four hours of notification. Engineering managers are responsible for initiating access review when a team member changes roles. Quarterly access reviews are conducted for all production and privileged systems. The IAM team generates access reports for each engineering manager, who must certify that all listed access is still required. Certifications are due within ten business days of the report being issued. Credential Management Passwords must meet the following minimum requirements: fourteen characters in length, combination of uppercase letters, lowercase letters, numbers, and symbols. Passwords may not be reused within the previous twenty-four password changes. Passwords must not be shared. The company-provided password manager is the required tool for storing and generating passwords. Multi-factor authentication is required for all systems without exception. Hardware security keys are required for access to production cloud accounts, the code repository, and the secrets manager. Time-based one-time password authenticators are acceptable for other systems where hardware keys are not supported. SMS-based two-factor authentication is not permitted for any production system access. API keys and tokens are credentials and must be treated as such. They must be

Software installed outside of the IT-approved catalog requires a security review, completed by submitting a request through the IT portal. Unapproved software discovered on company devices is removed without notice. Loss or damage must be reported to the IT Help Desk within 24 hours. Damage attributable to negligence may be charged to the employee at the depreciated value of the equipment. Theft requires a police report filed within 24 hours. Employees accessing Apex systems from personal devices must enroll the device in mobile device management and use only approved applications to access company data. Unenrolled personal devices are blocked from accessing internal systems. Confidentiality and Data Handling All employees sign a confidentiality and invention assignment agreement at hire. The agreement covers customer data, technical designs, business plans, financial information, and any information designated as confidential by a manager or the legal team. Customer data may only be accessed from company-managed devices and through approved channels. Screenshots, exports, or copies of customer data to personal devices or unauthorized cloud storage are strictly prohibited. Production database access requires a separate access request and is logged and audited. Queries to production data must be reviewed by a peer and documented with a business justification. Violations of data handling policy are reported to the Chief Information Security Officer and are subject to disciplinary action up to and including termination. Violations that may constitute a breach of customer data trigger the incident response plan and may require regulatory notification. Attendance and Absence Unplanned absences must be reported to the direct manager before the start of the workday via Slack or email. If the manager is unreachable, the team channel should be notified. Three consecutive unexplained absences are treated as voluntary job abandonment, and the employment relationship is considered terminated. Employees who anticipate a recurring pattern of lateness or absence due to a medical condition, disability, or family care obligation should contact the HR Business Partner to discuss an accommodation or an adjusted schedule before the pattern becomes a performance issue. Termination and Resignation Employees are asked to provide two weeks of written notice delivered to their manager and HR. Apex may accept notice and elect to end employment sooner with compensation through the notice period, or Apex may ask the employee to remain active through the full notice period depending on business need. Senior and technical roles may be asked to provide additional transition documentation. HR schedules an exit interview with the HR Business Partner on the final day of employment or via video call for remote employees. The exit interview covers departure reasons, feedback about the team and management experience, and transition status. Company equipment, physical badges, and credentials are collected or remotely wiped during the offboarding session. Final pay, including any accrued and unused PTO, is disbursed in accordance with applicable state law, typically on the next standard pay date or sooner if required. Employees with outstanding expense reports should submit them before their final day to ensure reimbursement. Policy questions should be directed

Employees submit receipts through the expense management system under the fitness reimbursement category. Reimbursement is processed with the regular monthly expense cycle. This benefit is separate from and in addition to the wellness stipend. Open Enrollment Open enrollment takes place each year during November, typically for a two-week period. During open enrollment, employees may make changes to their medical, dental, and vision elections, adjust FSA contribution amounts, and enroll in or change supplemental life or disability coverage without a qualifying life event. Changes made during open enrollment take effect January 1 of the following plan year. Outside of open enrollment, benefit elections may only be changed within thirty days of a qualifying life event. Qualifying life events include: marriage or divorce, birth or adoption of a child, death of a dependent, loss of coverage under another plan, a dependent losing eligibility, or a change in employment status that affects benefits eligibility. Documentation of the qualifying life event must be submitted to People Operations within thirty days of the event. Retroactive changes are not permitted after the thirty-day window. Benefits are elected through the benefits administration portal. Employees who do not make an election during open enrollment are automatically re-enrolled in their prior year elections for medical, dental, and vision coverage at the new premium rates. FSA elections do not roll over automatically; employees must make a new FSA election each year. Employees who wish to waive medical coverage must complete a waiver form each plan year. Employees who waive medical coverage receive a monthly wellness contribution of $75 added to their paycheck in lieu of the employer medical contribution. People Operations conducts a benefits information session during open enrollment each year and maintains a benefits FAQ on the company intranet. Questions about plan details, eligibility, or claims issues should be directed to People Operations or to the carrier's member services line. Apex Systems Budget Policy Fiscal Year 2026 — Effective January 1, 2026 This policy defines how annual and quarterly budgets are constructed, approved, managed, and monitored at Apex Systems. It applies to every cost center owner, functional leader, finance business partner, and any employee with purchase authority. Understanding this policy is a prerequisite for any employee who manages a budget line, approves vendor contracts, or initiates headcount requests. Budget Cycle and Calendar The fiscal year at Apex runs from January 1 through December 31. Budget planning follows a structured annual calendar designed to give functional leaders enough time to build thoughtful plans while giving finance enough time to consolidate, stress-test, and present a credible plan to the board. September 1: The CFO issues top-down guidance to each functional leader. The guidance specifies the target revenue growth rate for the year, the total headcount budget by function, and the maximum operating expense envelope. It is not a blank check for functional leaders to fill — it is a constraint to plan within. September 1 to October 1: Functional leaders conduct internal planning sessions. Each leader works with their finance business partner to identify the investments required to meet the revenue and efficiency targets. Decisions made during this window should be grounded in concrete business cases, not aspirational wish lists. October 15: Functional leaders submit their bottoms-up plans to the finance business partner. Plans are submitted in the standardized budget template, which includes: personnel cost by level and role, software and tools cost by vendor, professional services, facilities and travel, and a narrative memo explaining how the investments connect to company objectives. October 15 to November 15: Finance consolidates the submissions, identifies overlaps and gaps, runs sensitivity analyses, and produces the integrated company plan. Finance schedules individual working sessions with each functional leader to reconcile where the bottoms-up plan exceeds the top-down envelope. November 15 to December 5: The senior leadership team reviews the integrated plan in two sessions, one focused on revenue and go-to-market, one focused on operating expense and headcount. Material trade-offs are surfaced for CEO-level decision. December board meeting: The board of directors reviews and approves the final annual plan, including the total headcount target, the revenue plan, the operating expense budget, and the capital expenditure budget. December 20: Functional leaders receive their approved cost center budgets and are expected to communicate the plan to their teams before January 1. Mid-Year Reforecasts In addition to the annual plan, Apex produces two formal reforecasts per year. Q2 reforecast (June): Based on actual results through May and a revised assessment of second-half performance, the Q2 reforecast updates the full-year outlook for revenue, operating expense, headcount additions, and ending cash balance. The Q2 reforecast may result in approved budget adjustments for functions that are tracking significantly ahead of or behind plan. Q3 reforecast (September): The Q3 reforecast is the last opportunity to revise the current-year outlook before the annual planning cycle begins again. It also serves as a first look at the following year, providing early signals about where investment levels should increase or

an office requirement must be requested in writing and require VP approval and a documented business case. Requests are reviewed within 10 business days. Approved remote arrangements are revisited annually. Onboarding New hires complete a structured 30-60-90 day plan developed by HR in collaboration with the hiring manager before the employee's start date. The plan is shared with the new hire on day one and updated collaboratively throughout the first quarter. During week one, new hires complete security awareness training, data handling compliance modules, and role-specific access provisioning. They are introduced to their buddy — a volunteer colleague outside of the immediate team — who provides an informal guide to working at Apex. By day 30, each new hire should have a working understanding of the product, have met every member of their immediate team, and have shipped one measurable deliverable, however small. By day 60, they should be operating independently on their core responsibilities with periodic check-ins. By day 90, they should be contributing at the level expected for their role and level. HR schedules a formal 90-day check-in with the HR Business Partner for the department. The check-in covers performance against the 30-60-90 plan, integration into the team, and any concerns the employee or manager has not yet raised formally. Feedback from new hires is aggregated quarterly to identify onboarding process improvements. Performance Reviews Formal reviews occur twice per year: a mid-year review in March and an annual review in September. The September review is the primary cycle for compensation decisions and promotion approvals. The March review is developmental; it does not carry compensation changes but may trigger an early promotion if the case is exceptional. The review process begins three weeks before the review meeting. Employees complete a self-evaluation covering accomplishments against their goals, demonstration of core values, and growth opportunities. Managers solicit feedback from four to eight peer reviewers and one or two cross-functional partners identified by the employee and the manager jointly. Peer feedback is collected through the Lattice platform and is confidential to the manager; summaries, not verbatim quotes, are shared in the review. Managers write a calibrated narrative that incorporates self-evaluation, peer feedback, and the manager's direct observations. The narrative is reviewed by the manager's manager and, for Director and above roles, by the HR Business Partner before delivery. Calibration sessions across teams at the same level ensure ratings are consistent. Ratings follow a five-point scale: exceptional, exceeds expectations, meets expectations, developing, and unsatisfactory. The expected distribution for a healthy, well-hired team is roughly 10% exceptional, 25% exceeds expectations, 55% meets expectations, 8% developing, and 2% unsatisfactory. Managers whose teams deviate significantly from this distribution are asked to explain the deviation at calibration. Performance improvement plans are initiated by HR when an employee receives an unsatisfactory rating or fails to improve from a developing rating after two consecutive review cycles. A PIP specifies measurable goals, a support plan, and a timeline of 60 to 90 days. Completion of a PIP does not guarantee continued employment but does demonstrate good-faith commitment from both parties. Professional Development

Vendors that will process, store, or transmit customer data or employee personal information must complete the vendor security assessment administered by the Information Security team. Vendors that fail the security assessment may not be contracted until the identified risks are remediated or risk-accepted by the CISO. The procurement team maintains a vendor registry. Signing a contract with a vendor not in the registry requires Finance to create a new vendor record, which includes beneficial ownership disclosure and payment instructions verified independently from the vendor invoice. Budget Transfers and Amendments Within a cost center, transfers between natural accounts of up to $10,000 may be approved by the cost center owner in the accounting system. Transfers between natural accounts exceeding $10,000 require finance business partner approval and a brief justification noting why the original budget coding was incorrect or why business needs have changed. Transfers between cost centers — regardless of amount — require approval from both cost center owners and the finance business partner for the originating center. Cross-department transfers exceeding $50,000 require CFO approval. Budget transfers are recorded in the budget change log maintained by Finance and are reflected in the monthly reporting. Monitoring, Reporting, and Variance Management Finance business partners publish monthly actual-versus-budget reports for each cost center on the fifth business day after the accounting close. The report shows the current-month and year-to-date actuals versus plan, the full-year outlook based on current run rate, and the ending cash position. Cost center owners are expected to review the report within three business days of publication and submit a variance memo for any line item that is more than 10% or $25,000 off plan, whichever threshold is greater. Variance memos are submitted through the Finance portal and are reviewed by the finance business partner before the monthly finance leadership review. The monthly finance leadership review, attended by the CFO, VP Finance, FP&A lead, and all finance business partners, summarizes variances across cost centers, identifies emerging risks, and approves any course-correction actions. Non-Compliance Expenses submitted outside of policy are returned to the requester for correction before payment is processed. Recurring non-compliance — defined as three or more policy violations by the same employee within a 12-month period — is escalated to the employee's manager and HR Business Partner as a performance matter. Deliberate circumvention of spend authority controls, including splitting transactions to fall below approval thresholds, is treated as a serious conduct violation and may result in termination.

The template requires: a concise incident summary, an impact statement with quantified customer and business effect, a detailed timeline, a root cause analysis, a list of contributing factors, and a set of action items with named owners and target dates. Action items are created as Jira tickets with the label postmortem-action. They appear in the monthly reliability review dashboard. Action items that are not resolved within 30 days are escalated to the engineering manager for the responsible team. Change Freeze Apex observes a mandatory change freeze from December 20 through January 2 each year to protect customers during the end-of-year period. Additionally, change freezes may be declared by the VP of Engineering for periods surrounding major customer events such as contract renewals, audit windows, or penetration tests. During a change freeze, only rollbacks and security-critical changes may be deployed. Security-critical changes are those that address a confirmed actively exploited vulnerability or correct a data exposure. Each such deployment requires written approval from the VP of Engineering, documented in the exceptions log with a severity justification and a description of the change.

After the deductible is met, cost-sharing mirrors the PPO: Tier 1 generic drugs are $10 per thirty-day supply, Tier 2 preferred brand drugs are $35, Tier 3 non-preferred brand drugs are $70, and specialty drugs are subject to twenty percent coinsurance with a $150 per-prescription maximum. Monthly Premium Contributions for the HDHP Employee only: Employee pays $30, Company pays $390. Employee plus spouse or domestic partner: Employee pays $90, Company pays $680. Employee plus children: Employee pays $75, Company pays $625. Employee plus family: Employee pays $145, Company pays $920. Health Savings Account The HDHP is designed to be used in combination with a Health Savings Account. The HSA is a personal, tax-advantaged savings account that can only be opened and contributed to while enrolled in an IRS-qualifying high-deductible health plan. Employees enrolled in the Apex Systems HDHP are automatically eligible to open and fund an HSA. The HSA provides three distinct tax advantages, sometimes called the triple tax benefit. First, contributions made through payroll deductions are pre-tax, reducing your taxable income for the year. Second, funds in the HSA grow tax-free — any interest or investment returns accumulate without being taxed. Third, withdrawals for qualified medical expenses are tax-free at any time. Together, these three benefits make the HSA one of the most tax-efficient savings vehicles available. IRS contribution limits for 2026: $4,300 for individual coverage, $8,550 for family coverage. Employees age fifty-five and older may make an additional $1,000 catch-up contribution, for a total of $5,300 (individual) or $9,550 (family). These limits include both employee and employer contributions combined. Apex Systems makes an annual employer contribution to the HSA of $500 for employees with individual HDHP coverage and $1,000 for employees with family HDHP coverage. This contribution is deposited in January of the plan year. Employees who enroll in the HDHP mid-year due to a qualifying life event or as a new hire receive a prorated employer contribution based on the number of months remaining in the plan year. Employees may contribute up to the IRS annual limit themselves, subject to the combined employee-plus-employer cap. After accounting for the Apex Systems employer HSA contribution, an employee with individual coverage has $3,800 of personal contribution room remaining in 2026 ($4,300 IRS limit minus the $500 employer contribution). An employee with family coverage has $7,550 remaining ($8,550 minus $1,000). HSA funds roll over from year to year without limit or forfeiture. There is no use-it-or-lose-it requirement. Balances can accumulate across years and be invested once the account balance exceeds a threshold set by the HSA administrator. Many employees use the HSA as a long-term healthcare savings vehicle, paying current medical expenses out of pocket while allowing HSA balances to grow for future use, including healthcare costs in retirement. The HSA is owned by the employee, not by Apex Systems. If you leave

Vague positive feedback ("great to work with, always helpful") is less useful than specific examples. Peer reviews are confidential: the manager reads the peer feedback but the employee does not see individual peer responses. The manager synthesizes peer themes and includes them anonymously in the manager assessment. Confidentiality does not mean immunity from accountability. If a peer review contains information suggesting misconduct or policy violations, People Operations may follow up. Manager Assessment The manager writes the formal performance assessment after reviewing the employee's self-evaluation and peer feedback. The assessment is the primary document that defines the employee's performance for the year. It must be evidence-based, specific, and balanced. Manager assessment sections: Overall performance summary: A two to three paragraph narrative summary of the employee's performance during the year. This section should synthesize the most significant contributions, the quality of the employee's work, their growth relative to prior performance, and any notable gaps. Goal achievement: For each goal set during the year, the manager assesses the degree to which the goal was achieved and the quality of the work. This section should reference specific outcomes and, where appropriate, note circumstances that affected performance. Strengths: Two to four specific, evidence-based strengths observed during the year. Each strength should be supported by at least one concrete example. Development areas: One to two areas for improvement with specific, actionable recommendations. Development areas should be framed constructively and focused on what the employee can do differently, not on personality or fixed traits. Peer feedback themes: A summary of themes from peer reviews, presented anonymously. The manager identifies patterns across peer responses — both positive themes and development themes — and includes them in the assessment. Values alignment: An assessment of how the employee's behavior and work demonstrated Apex Systems values. Apex Systems Incident Response Runbook Effective January 1, 2026 This document defines the incident management process for Apex Systems production systems. It covers severity classification, the on-call rotation, escalation procedures, communication requirements, postmortem standards, and SLA targets. All members of the Engineering organization are expected to be familiar with this runbook. On-call engineers are required to review it before taking their first shift. Definition of a Production Incident A production incident is any unplanned event that causes or risks causing degradation or unavailability of a customer-facing system, loss or corruption of customer data, a breach of security controls, or a violation of a contractual or regulatory SLA. Incidents do not include planned maintenance, feature flags, or phased rollouts that behave as expected. All incidents must be logged in PagerDuty and tracked to resolution. Severity Levels All incidents are classified into one of four severity levels. Classification is made by the incident commander based on observed and reasonably anticipated customer impact. The severity level determines response urgency, required communications, and escalation thresholds. P0 — Critical: Complete outage of a core customer-facing service, sustained for more than two minutes. Examples include the API returning 5xx errors for all requests, inability for any customer to authenticate, data loss or corruption affecting multiple customers, or a confirmed security breach involving customer data. A P0 requires an incident commander and a communications lead to be assigned immediately. The incident commander must page the on-call infrastructure lead and engineering manager. The CEO and CTO must be notified within fifteen minutes of P0 declaration. Customer-facing communication must be posted to the status page within thirty minutes. SLA target for P0 resolution is four hours. If not resolved within two hours, executive escalation is mandatory. P1 — High: Significant degradation of a core customer-facing service, or a complete outage of a non-core service. Examples include elevated error rates above five percent on a customer-facing API, a payment processing failure affecting a subset of customers, latency degradation above three times the normal p99 baseline sustained for more than five minutes, or a security event with potential customer data exposure. A P1 requires an incident commander. Engineering management must be notified within thirty minutes of P1 declaration. A status page update must be posted within one hour. SLA target for P1 resolution is eight hours. P2 — Medium: Partial or intermittent degradation of non-critical functionality, with no confirmed customer impact or with impact limited to a small subset of users. Examples include elevated error rates on internal APIs, latency degradation above two times baseline on secondary services, or a failed deployment that did not reach production. P2 incidents do not require a dedicated incident commander but must be assigned to a named engineer responsible for investigation and resolution. SLA target for P2 resolution is twenty-four hours. P3 — Low: Minor issues that do not affect customers and have a known workaround. Examples include non-critical monitoring alerts, cosmetic bugs in internal tooling, or log pipeline delays. P3 incidents are tracked in the issue tracker and resolved during normal business hours. SLA target for

Mental Health and Employee Assistance Program Apex Systems provides a comprehensive Employee Assistance Program at no cost to all employees and their household members regardless of medical plan enrollment. for the requested operation receive HTTP 403 Forbidden. The error response body follows the standard error model described below. Rate Limits The API enforces rate limits at the tenant level. Default limits apply to all tenants unless overridden by a plan-level or contract-level configuration. Default limits: 600 requests per minute across all API keys and access tokens combined, with a burst allowance of 60 requests in any single second. Ingestion endpoints have a separate limit of 100 requests per minute to protect downstream Kafka throughput. Query endpoints have a concurrency limit of 10 simultaneous in-flight queries per tenant. Enterprise plan limits: 3,000 requests per minute and 200 simultaneous queries. Higher limits are available with advance notice; contact your account manager. When the rate limit is reached, the API returns HTTP 429 Too Many Requests. The response includes three headers: X-RateLimit-Limit (the configured limit), X-RateLimit-Remaining (the number of requests remaining in the current window), and Retry-After (the number of seconds until the limit resets). Clients must respect the Retry-After value. Ignoring the retry guidance and polling at high frequency during a rate-limit period may result in temporary IP-level blocking. All SDKs maintained by Apex implement exponential backoff with full jitter starting from the Retry-After value. Third-party integrations should implement the same. Error Model All error responses conform to RFC 7807 Problem Details. The response body is a JSON object with the following fields: type: a URI that identifies the error class, such as https://api.apex-systems.example/errors/schema-validation-failed. These URIs are stable and may be used in client code for programmatic error handling. title: a human-readable summary of the problem type, consistent for all occurrences of the same type. status: the HTTP status code as an integer, included for convenience. detail: a human-readable description of the specific occurrence, including actionable guidance where applicable. instance: a URI that identifies the specific request, useful for tracing in logs. correlation_id: a UUID generated per request. Customers should include this in support tickets. Apex support uses it to locate the request trace in Datadog. For validation errors, an additional errors array is included, where each element identifies the field path that failed validation and the rule that was violated. Ingestion Endpoints POST /datasets/{dataset_id}/records Synchronously validates and asynchronously ingests a batch of JSON records. The request body is a JSON object with a records array of up to 1,000 individual records. Each record is validated against the dataset schema; records that fail validation are rejected and included in the error response without affecting the valid records in the same batch. The maximum size per record is 1 MB; the maximum total request body size is 50 MB. The response returns HTTP 202 Accepted with a body containing a submission_id (UUID), a submitted_count, a rejected_count, and an array of rejection details. The submission is processed asynchronously; the submission status can be polled using GET /submissions/{submission_id}. POST /datasets/{dataset_id}/files Creates a pre-signed S3 PUT URL for uploading a file directly to Apex storage without passing through the API servers. Accepted file formats are Parquet, CSV (UTF-8 with configurable delimiter), and newline-delimited JSON. Maximum file size

Sales Compensation Sales roles — Account Executive, Sales Development Representative, Sales Engineer, and Customer Success Manager — operate on individual sales compensation plans issued at the start of each fiscal year or at hire. Plans specify the base salary, on-target variable, quota, and accelerator and decelerator structure. Standard structures are either 50/50 base-to-variable for pure quota-carrying roles or 60/40 for roles with a mix of quota and relationship responsibilities. Commissions are calculated monthly against booked, signed, and invoiced revenue that meets qualification criteria defined in the plan. Accelerators apply at 100%, 120%, and 150% of quota attainment. Revenue booked above 150% of quarterly quota earns at 175% of the standard commission rate. Decelerators apply below 50% attainment in a given quarter, resulting in a 50% commission rate. The Finance and Revenue Operations teams administer commission calculations and publish results in Spiff by the fifth business day after month end. Plan disputes must be raised with the Revenue Operations team within 15 days of statement publication. Disputes filed after this window are reviewed at the company's discretion. Equity All full-time employees receive a stock option grant at hire. Options are priced at the fair market value established by the most recent 409A valuation conducted by a third-party qualified appraiser. Grants vest over four years: 25% vest after the first year of service (the one-year cliff), and the remaining 75% vest monthly over the following 36 months at a rate of 1/48th of the total grant per month. New-hire grant sizes by level for Tier 1 locations, as a percentage of options representing the fully diluted equity: L1 and L2: 0.01% to 0.04% L3 Senior: 0.05% to 0.10% L4 Staff: 0.10% to 0.20% L5 Principal: 0.20% to 0.40% L6 Director: 0.30% to 0.60% L7 Vice President: individually negotiated, typically 0.5% to 1.5% Refresh grants are awarded each September to employees in good standing, based on performance and tenure. Target refresh amounts equal approximately 25% of the annualized new-hire grant for the employee's current level. Refresh grants are subject to the same four-year vesting schedule with a one-year cliff. There is no limit on the number of refresh grants an employee may receive. Employees have 90 days following their termination date to exercise vested options. Unvested options are forfeited upon separation. Employees who resign with more than four years of service and at least 12 months remaining on their final refresh grant may request an extension of the exercise window to 12 months; this requires CEO approval. Employees rated developing typically receive no merit increase. Typical merit increase ranges by rating: exceptional — 8% to 12%; exceeds expectations — 5% to 8%; meets expectations — 3% to 5%; developing — 0%. Promotions carry

Specialty drugs are subject to twenty percent coinsurance with a $150 maximum per prescription. Apex Systems Deployment Guide Revision 2.1 — January 2026 This guide is the authoritative reference for shipping code to any environment operated by Apex Systems. It applies to every engineer who merges code to the monorepo, regardless of seniority or team. Deviations from this process require written approval from the VP of Engineering and are documented in the exceptions log. Environments Apex operates four environments with distinct purposes, access controls, and deployment rules. Local is the development environment that every engineer runs on their workstation. It is defined as a docker-compose profile in the monorepo root. Local spins up all services, an in-process Kafka broker, a PostgreSQL instance, a MinIO S3-compatible object store, and a lightweight Trino container. Engineers are expected to keep local fully functional; a broken local environment that is not fixed within 24 hours should be escalated to the DevEx team. Seed data scripts are included for each service so that engineers do not interact with staging data. Staging is a full Kubernetes environment hosted in us-east-1 that mirrors the production topology, including all three worker node groups, the full observability stack, and real SSL certificates. Staging serves synthetic data generated by a daily data generator job. Engineers use staging for integration testing, external demos, and validating infrastructure changes. Staging is shared across the team; destructive tests that affect shared state must be coordinated in the #staging-coordination Slack channel. Canary is a 5% traffic slice of the production cluster. A separate Kubernetes service revision is deployed, and traffic is split using Argo Rollouts with a weighted VirtualService in Istio. Canary exists to validate behavioral changes under real customer traffic before full promotion. Engineers may observe canary metrics in the Grafana canary dashboard, which is automatically pinned to the revision deployed. Production serves 100% of customer traffic across three regions. Deployment to production is fully automated after a successful canary period. Engineers do not deploy to production manually; the delivery pipeline handles promotion. Source Control and Code Review All services, libraries, infrastructure definitions, Helm charts, and CI configuration live in a single monorepo hosted on GitHub. The monorepo uses Bazel for builds with a shared build cache hosted on a dedicated S3 bucket. Bazel ensures that only affected targets are rebuilt and retested, which keeps CI times bounded even as the repository grows. Developers branch from main using the naming convention owner-username/APEX-ticket-number-short-description. Branches older than 30 days without an open pull request are automatically deleted by a nightly GitHub Actions workflow. Direct pushes to main are disallowed by branch protection rules. Every change requires a pull request. Pull requests require: at least one approval from a person listed in the CODEOWNERS file for the changed path, passing required status checks, no unresolved review comments marked as blocking, and no merge conflicts. Force-pushing to open PRs is disallowed. Approved PRs are merged using the squash-and-merge strategy to keep the main branch history linear. For changes that affect the API contract, the Helm values schema, the Kafka message schema, or the database migration files, a second approval from the platform-owners team is

Critical: A finding that indicates the absence or complete failure of a key control, presents an immediate risk of material financial misstatement, or indicates a material compliance violation. Critical findings are escalated immediately to the CFO and Audit Committee Chair and must have a remediation plan within fifteen business days. Critical findings that have not been remediated within sixty days are re-escalated to the full Audit Committee. High: A finding that indicates significant weakness in a key control that does not rise to the level of critical, or a pattern of exceptions in a key control. High findings must have a remediation plan within thirty business days and must be remediated within ninety days. Medium: A finding that indicates weakness in a non-key control, a minor exception pattern in a key control, or a policy compliance gap with limited financial or operational risk. Medium findings must be remediated within one hundred eighty days. Low: An observation or best-practice recommendation that does not represent a control deficiency. Low findings are tracked but do not have a mandatory remediation timeline. They are reviewed with management annually. Findings are recorded in the audit findings tracker and reviewed quarterly by the Internal Audit team and the relevant business area management. Findings that are past their remediation deadline are escalated to the department head and CFO. Repeat findings — the same finding appearing in two consecutive audits — are automatically elevated one severity level. Remediation Tracking Management is responsible for remediating all findings within the agreed timelines. The Internal Audit team tracks remediation status in the audit findings tracker. Each finding is assigned a named owner in management who is responsible for the remediation action. Remediation evidence is submitted by management through the audit findings tracker. The Internal Audit team reviews the evidence and validates remediation within ten business days of submission. Validation may include re-testing the affected control to confirm it is now operating effectively. Validated remediations are marked closed in the audit findings tracker. Findings where evidence is submitted but not sufficient to validate remediation are returned to management with a request for additional evidence. The deadline for the finding is not extended during the review period. The remediation status report is presented to the Audit Committee at each quarterly meeting, summarizing open findings by severity, age, and business area. External Auditor Relationship The external auditor provides an independent audit opinion on the annual consolidated financial statements and review opinions on each of the quarterly financial statements (except Q4, which is covered by the annual audit). The external auditor is selected and overseen by the Audit Committee, not management. Auditor Selection and Independence: The external auditor is subject to a competitive RFP process every five years. The lead audit partner rotates every five years. The Audit Committee evaluates the independence of the auditor annually by reviewing the auditor's independence letter and any non-audit services provided to the company. Non-audit services by the external auditor are subject to pre-approval by the Audit Committee. Prohibited non-audit services include bookkeeping, financial information systems design and implementation, human resources

Status Page Update — Progress: Headline: Update: [brief description of current status] Body: We have identified [root cause or probable cause if known]. We are [current action being taken]. Current impact: [describe]. Expected resolution: [timeframe or "we will update in X minutes"]. Status Page Update — Resolved: Headline: Resolved: [brief description of issue] Body: The issue affecting [service/feature] has been resolved as of [time UTC]. The root cause was [brief description]. We will share a full postmortem [within X days / link]. We apologize for the disruption. Slack Incident Declaration Template: :red_circle: INCIDENT DECLARED — [P0/P1] Service: [affected service] Impact: [describe customer impact] Status: Investigating Incident Commander: [@name] Technical Lead: [@name] Comms Lead: [@name] Incident Doc: [link] Postmortem Template All P0 and P1 postmortems must be completed and published within five business days of incident resolution. The postmortem is written by the technical lead or incident commander with contributions from all participants. Postmortems are blameless: they identify systemic causes, process gaps, and tooling failures. They do not assign personal fault. Postmortem sections: Incident Summary: One to three sentences describing what happened, when, and the customer impact. Timeline: A chronological log of events including detection, escalations, actions taken, and resolution. Each entry includes a timestamp in UTC and the name of the engineer or system responsible for the entry. Root Cause Analysis: A description of the technical root cause or causes. Use the five-whys technique to trace from the immediate cause back to the underlying systemic factor. Avoid stopping at the first proximate cause. Customer Impact: Quantified description of impact including number of affected customers or requests, duration of degradation, and whether any SLAs were breached. What Went Well: A list of things that worked as intended during the incident response. This section is not an excuse for what went wrong — it identifies practices and tools to preserve. What Could Be Improved: A list of specific gaps identified during the response. Be concrete. Action Items: A numbered list of specific, assigned action items with a named owner and a target completion date. Each action item should address one of the items identified in the previous section. Action items are tracked in Jira and reviewed at the weekly engineering sync. Postmortems are shared with the full Engineering organization and summarized for the customer-facing status page if the incident was

Suspension: In cases where the company needs to conduct an investigation or where a serious violation has occurred that may or may not warrant termination, an employee may be placed on paid administrative leave pending the outcome of the investigation. Termination: Termination is appropriate when prior disciplinary steps have not resulted in improvement, when the violation is too severe to address through progressive discipline, when the employee has engaged in dishonesty, harassment, violence or threats of violence, theft, serious security violations, or conduct that puts the company or its customers at risk, or when the employment relationship is otherwise untenable. Involuntary terminations are approved by the employee's manager, People Operations, and the department head. Terminations of director-level and above employees additionally require approval from the CEO. Mandatory Reporting and Zero Tolerance Certain conduct violations are treated as zero-tolerance matters that result in immediate termination without prior progressive discipline. These include: physical violence or threats of violence, sexual assault or sexual coercion, theft or embezzlement of company funds, willful destruction of company property, falsification of financial records, disclosure of material non-public financial information, and possession of weapons on company premises. Managers who witness or receive a report of a zero-tolerance violation must immediately escalate to People Operations and the General Counsel. No manager may unilaterally decide not to escalate a potential zero-tolerance violation. Acknowledgment and Annual Training All employees are required to read and acknowledge this Code of Conduct upon hire and annually thereafter. Acknowledgment is recorded in the HRIS. Annual training on the key provisions of this Code, including the anti-harassment policy, conflict of interest policy, and reporting procedures, is required for all employees and must be completed within thirty days of the annual rollout. New hires must complete the training within their first fourteen days. Managers have an elevated responsibility under this Code. In addition to the obligations above, managers must create and maintain an environment in which employees feel safe reporting concerns, must not engage in or permit retaliatory conduct, and must escalate reports of potential misconduct promptly. Failure by a manager to uphold these elevated obligations is itself a disciplinary matter.

Any image layer that contains a secret is considered compromised and triggers a rotation incident. An accidental commit of a plaintext secret to the repository triggers an automated response: the secret is flagged by the GitHub secret scanning integration, the rotation runbook is triggered, and an incident is created in PagerDuty. Rollback Procedures Every deployment records an immutable Helm release history in the cluster. Argo CD maintains the complete sync history for each application. A rollback in Argo CD reverts to the previous stable revision within approximately 60 seconds and is the preferred remediation for any regression introduced by a new deployment. Database migrations are managed by golang-migrate. Migrations must be backward compatible with the previous application revision. This means that before removing a column, the application must stop reading from it; before renaming a column, the application must support both names. A migration that is not backward compatible must be staged across multiple releases with explicit coordination between the migration author and the on-call team. If a rollback cannot resolve an incident because the migration is already applied and the previous revision does not support the new schema, the incident commander convenes the relevant service owners to write a forward-fix migration and an application patch. This scenario is considered a process failure and is included in the postmortem. On-Call Operations Each service domain has a named primary and secondary on-call engineer in PagerDuty. Rotations are weekly, starting Monday at 09:00 Pacific. Engineers must acknowledge a PagerDuty alert within 5 minutes. Failure to acknowledge triggers escalation to the secondary. Failure of the secondary to acknowledge within an additional 5 minutes escalates to the on-call engineering manager. Severity 1: customer-impacting service degradation or outage. Requires an incident commander, a dedicated Slack incident channel, and a live status page update within 15 minutes. Resolution is the only priority. Severity 2: degraded service that is not causing direct customer impact but poses a risk of escalation. Addressed during business hours unless the risk assessment changes. Severity 3: non-customer-impacting internal alerts, performance regressions, or elevated error rates within acceptable bounds. Tracked in Jira and addressed in the next sprint. On-call engineers are compensated with a $200 per week on-call stipend regardless of whether any incidents occur. Incidents that require significant out-of-hours work — defined as more than two hours outside of normal working hours in a single week — are compensated with one additional day of PTO. Incident Management and Postmortems All severity 1 and severity 2 incidents require a blameless postmortem document. The postmortem must be published in the internal wiki within five business days of incident resolution. The template requires: a concise incident summary, an impact statement with quantified customer and business effect, a detailed timeline, a root cause analysis, a list of contributing factors, and a set of action items with named owners and target dates. Action items are created as Jira tickets with the label postmortem-action. They appear in

Applications that meet minimum qualifications are moved to the review stage. The recruiter shares a shortlist of ten to fifteen candidates with the hiring manager, who selects candidates for the initial recruiter screen. The recruiter screen is a thirty-minute video or phone call conducted by the recruiter. The purpose is to confirm the candidate's interest in the role, validate basic qualifications, discuss compensation expectations, and assess communication and professionalism. The recruiter uses a standardized set of questions drawn from the role's competency framework. After each screen, the recruiter logs notes in the applicant tracking system and makes a pass or no-pass recommendation to the hiring manager. Candidates who do not pass the recruiter screen are notified within three business days by email. Candidates who pass are moved to the hiring manager screen. Hiring Manager Screen The hiring manager screen is a forty-five-minute video call between the candidate and the hiring manager. The purpose is to explore the candidate's background and experience in more depth, assess alignment with team needs, and give the candidate a clear picture of the role and team. The hiring manager should review the candidate's resume and the recruiter's notes before the call. Hiring managers are expected to ask consistent, competency-based questions for all candidates interviewing for the same role. People Operations maintains a question bank organized by competency and level that hiring managers must draw from. Follow-up or clarifying questions are permitted. Hypothetical or brain-teaser questions that are not grounded in real work scenarios are discouraged. Questions about protected characteristics including age, marital status, family plans, religion, national origin, and disability are prohibited. After the hiring manager screen, the hiring manager provides a written recommendation within twenty-four hours through the applicant tracking system. Candidates who advance proceed to the formal interview loop. Technical Interview For engineering, data, and product roles, a technical interview is required before the full panel interview loop. The technical interview is designed and administered by the relevant technical team. For software engineering roles, the technical interview consists of two parts: a sixty-minute coding exercise conducted via a shared coding environment, and a thirty-minute systems design or architecture discussion appropriate to the seniority level of the role. Technical interviewers must submit written feedback in the applicant tracking system within twenty-four hours of the interview. The feedback must address the specific technical competencies assessed, with a clear pass or no-pass recommendation. Contributions were solid, goals were substantially achieved, and the employee operated effectively as a member of the team. This is the most common rating and reflects good, reliable performance. Employees who consistently meet expectations are performing well. Developing: The employee met expectations in some areas but fell short in others. There are one or more significant gaps in performance, skill, or values alignment that must be addressed. A Developing rating triggers a formal development plan for the following year. Employees with a Developing rating are not eligible for a merit increase and are not eligible for promotion in the subsequent cycle. Not Meeting Expectations: The employee did not meet expectations in multiple significant areas. This rating is reserved for cases of sustained underperformance or significant misconduct. A Not Meeting Expectations rating results in a mandatory Performance Improvement Plan. Continued failure to improve following a PIP results in termination. Employees receiving this rating must be discussed with People Operations before the rating is finalized. Calibration Sessions Calibration sessions are held after manager assessments are drafted but before ratings are finalized. The purpose of calibration is to ensure consistency of ratings across the organization, to surface bias, and to ensure that the distribution of ratings reflects actual performance rather than managerial generosity or severity. Calibration is facilitated by People Operations and attended by all managers within a department and the department head. Each manager presents their proposed ratings for their direct reports, summarizes the key evidence supporting each rating, and takes questions from other managers and the department head. The group discusses cases where ratings seem high or low relative to the evidence presented, where ratings for employees at the same level appear inconsistent, or where a manager's rating distribution appears skewed compared to other managers. Managers may change proposed ratings following calibration. Final ratings must be approved by the department head and People Operations. Employees are not present during calibration. Managers must not share the content of calibration discussions with employees. Calibration is particularly important for borderline cases — employees proposed for Exceptional or Not Meeting Expectations ratings, employees proposed for promotion, and employees at the same level where there are large rating disparities. Managers who disagree with a calibration outcome may escalate to the Chief People Officer, whose decision is final. Performance Improvement Plans A Performance Improvement Plan is required for any employee receiving a Developing or Not Meeting Expectations rating. The PIP is a formal document that defines specific performance gaps, sets measurable improvement goals, establishes a timeline for improvement, and describes the consequences of continued underperformance. PIP development: The manager drafts the PIP in consultation with People Operations. The PIP must include: a description of the specific performance gaps with examples, the performance standard that is expected, the specific and measurable goals the employee must achieve, the timeline (typically thirty to ninety

Meetings include time with the Head of Internal Audit and the lead external audit partner without management present. The CFO and Controller attend Audit Committee meetings by invitation and present materials on financial reporting and control matters. Meeting minutes are taken and approved at the following meeting. Responsibilities: The Audit Committee is responsible for: overseeing the integrity of financial reporting; reviewing and approving the annual financial statements before publication; selecting, compensating, and overseeing the external auditor; reviewing the scope and results of external audit work; overseeing the internal audit function including approving the annual audit plan; reviewing significant accounting policies and estimates; overseeing the company's risk management framework; and reviewing and investigating concerns reported through the whistleblower hotline. Annual Internal Audit Plan The Internal Audit team prepares an annual audit plan each fall covering the following fiscal year. The plan is based on a risk assessment that considers: the inherent risk of each auditable entity based on financial materiality, complexity, regulatory exposure, and rate of change; the results of prior audits and the status of previously identified findings; input from the Audit Committee, CFO, and department heads on emerging risks; and external developments including new accounting standards, regulatory changes, and industry risk trends. The risk assessment produces a ranked list of auditable entities. The annual plan allocates available audit resources to the highest-ranked entities. The plan is presented to the Audit Committee in November for approval before the start of the new fiscal year. Approved plans include the audit scope, the timing of each audit engagement, the estimated hours and resources, and the expected deliverable for each engagement. Mid-year adjustments to the plan require Audit Committee approval. Unplanned audits may be commissioned by the Audit Committee or CFO at any time. Audit Engagement Process Each internal audit engagement follows a standard methodology: Planning Phase: The audit team develops a planning memo that documents the scope and objectives of the audit, the key risks being addressed, the control framework being evaluated, the data sources and systems to be reviewed, the audit procedures to be performed, and the timeline. The planning memo is reviewed by the Head of Internal Audit and shared with the relevant business area management as a courtesy before fieldwork begins. Management input may be requested but management may not modify the scope of audit objectives. Fieldwork Phase: Auditors execute the procedures defined in the planning memo. Procedures include walkthroughs with process owners, review of policy and procedure documentation, review of system access and configuration, transactional testing using statistical or risk-based sample selection, analytical procedures, and reconciliation testing. All audit evidence is documented in the working paper files and retained for seven years. Auditors record observations throughout fieldwork, including preliminary findings identified during testing. Material subsequent events — transactions or developments that occur between the balance sheet date and the financial statement issuance date and that are significant to users — are disclosed in a subsequent events note. Revenue Recognition Apex recognizes revenue in accordance with ASC 606, Revenue from Contracts with Customers. Apex Systems Compensation Guide Effective January 1, 2026 This guide explains how Apex Systems structures base pay, variable compensation, equity, and benefits for all full-time employees. It is distributed at hire, republished annually after the September compensation review, and updated mid-year if material changes are made. Managers are encouraged to walk through this guide with new team members during the first month of employment. Compensation Philosophy Apex targets the 65th percentile of base salary for our peer group, defined as venture-backed enterprise software companies with between 200 and 2,000 employees and annual recurring revenue between $50 million and $500 million, primarily based in the United States. Total target compensation — including base salary, target annual bonus, and the annualized Black-Scholes value of equity grants — is positioned at the 75th percentile of the same peer group. Pay is benchmarked twice per year using Radford Global Technology Survey data, with a supplemental reference to Levels.fyi for engineering roles to capture real-world market movement in technical talent. When survey data and market anecdotes diverge by more than 10% at a given level, HR initiates an ad hoc market review before the next scheduled calibration. The philosophy is grounded in the belief that compensating people well relative to the market reduces voluntary attrition, eliminates a significant source of distraction, and allows the company to attract and retain people who would otherwise join larger competitors. Career Levels and Pay Bands Apex operates a single unified level framework that applies across all functions — engineering, product, design, sales, finance, HR, legal, and operations. The seven levels and their approximate base salary ranges for Tier 1 locations are: L1 Associate: $70,000 to $90,000. Entry-level roles. Typically 0 to 2 years of professional experience. Supervised closely with structured deliverables. L2 Specialist: $90,000 to $120,000. Independently executes well-defined tasks. Typically 2 to 4 years of experience. Contributes to team planning and improves their own work quality without being asked. L3 Senior: $120,000 to $160,000. Drives outcomes for a defined scope of work with limited oversight. Mentors L1 and L2 colleagues informally. Typically 4 to 8 years of experience. This is the career level where most employees spend the majority of their tenure. L4 Staff: $160,000 to $210,000. Owns complex cross-team problems and drives alignment across peers. Influences roadmap and architecture. Typically requires significant domain expertise and strong cross-functional communication. L5 Principal: $210,000 to $280,000. Sets technical or functional direction for a broad domain. Operates with VP-level influence and visibility. Expected to develop L3 and L4 talent. Fewer than 5% of employees reach this level. L6 Director: $250,000 to $350,000. Manages teams or functions, sets strategy, represents the domain to senior leadership. Accountable for team performance, hiring, and culture. L7 Vice President: $350,000 and above. Leads a major function or business unit. Member of the senior leadership team. Compensation for L7 is individually negotiated with CEO approval and CFO review. Each band has a published minimum, midpoint, and maximum. Apex Systems Hiring Process Effective January 1, 2026 This document describes the end-to-end hiring process at Apex Systems, from the decision to open a new role through the candidate's first day of employment. It applies to all hiring managers, the People Operations team, recruiters, and any employee who participates in interviews or hiring decisions. The goal of this process is to identify, assess, and hire candidates who are technically qualified, aligned with Apex Systems values, and positioned to contribute meaningfully to their team from day one. Headcount Planning and Role Authorization All new hire requests must originate from an approved headcount plan. Headcount plans are submitted by department leaders during the annual budgeting process each November and approved by the Chief Executive Officer and Chief Financial Officer before the start of the new fiscal year. Mid-cycle additions to approved headcount require a written business case submitted to the department head and CFO. The business case must include the role description, expected start date, compensation band, and the business impact of leaving the position unfilled. Once headcount is authorized, the hiring manager submits a Job Requisition Form to People Operations. The form captures the job title, reporting relationship, level, compensation band, preferred start date, target candidate profile, and any specific technical requirements. People Operations will acknowledge the requisition within two business days and assign a recruiter. Job Description Development The recruiter works with the hiring manager to develop a job description within five business days of requisition acknowledgment. Job descriptions must include the role title, department, reporting structure, a summary of the role, a list of core responsibilities, required qualifications, preferred qualifications, and the compensation range. Apex Systems posts salary ranges on all external job postings in compliance with applicable pay transparency laws. Job descriptions are reviewed by the hiring manager, the department head, and People Operations before posting. Engineering roles additionally require review by the relevant technical lead to confirm accuracy of technical requirements. People Operations maintains a library of base job description templates that may be adapted for each requisition. Job Posting and Candidate Sourcing Approved job descriptions are posted on the Apex Systems careers page, LinkedIn, and any relevant industry-specific job boards selected by the recruiter. Positions are posted for a minimum of ten business days before the recruiter presents a shortlist to the hiring manager, unless the hiring manager requests a faster timeline due to business urgency. Internal candidates are encouraged to apply. Any employee who has been in their current role for at least six months and has received a satisfactory performance rating in their most recent review is eligible to apply for open positions. Internal applicants are notified of their status within five business days of application. Referrals from current employees are tracked through the applicant tracking system, and referring employees are eligible for the referral bonus outlined in the compensation guide once the referred candidate completes ninety days of employment. Passive candidate sourcing is also permitted. Recruiters and hiring managers may reach out directly to candidates through LinkedIn and professional networks. All candidate communications must be

A PIP specifies measurable goals, a support plan, and a timeline of 60 to 90 days.
