Professional Development Every employee receives an annual learning and development budget of $2,000, effective on their hire anniversary date. The budget may be applied to external conferences, online courses, in-person training, books, professional certifications, or executive coaching. Manager approval is required for individual expenses above $500. The budget does not carry over from year to year and cannot be cashed out. Apex sponsors internal knowledge sharing through Friday Tech Talks, a 45-minute session hosted by a volunteer employee or external speaker every Friday at noon Pacific. Recordings are posted to the internal learning library. The company holds an annual Engineering Summit in Q3, which all engineers and product managers are expected to attend regardless of location. Employees pursuing advanced degrees relevant to their role may apply for tuition reimbursement of up to $5,250 per year, the IRS excludable maximum. Applications are reviewed by the HR Business Partner and require evidence of enrollment and a grade of B or better for reimbursement. Code of Conduct Apex is committed to a workplace free from harassment, discrimination, and retaliation. These behaviors are grounds for immediate termination regardless of seniority. Protected characteristics include race, color, religion, sex, national origin, age, disability, genetic information, sexual orientation, gender identity, and veteran status. Apex maintains an anonymous reporting channel, EthicsPoint, operated by a third-party vendor. Reports are reviewed by the Head of HR and the General Counsel. All complaints are investigated within five business days by an HR Business Partner not associated with the reporting chain. The outcome of the investigation is communicated to the reporting party to the extent permitted by law. Employees who witness misconduct are encouraged to report it. Bystander inaction, particularly by people managers, is treated as a contributing factor in misconduct investigations. Conflicts of interest must be disclosed to the HR Business Partner. Common conflicts include ownership stakes in competitor or customer companies, outside board seats, and employment of immediate family members. Disclosed conflicts are reviewed and mitigated, not automatically disqualifying. Equipment and IT Apex provides a company laptop, external monitor, keyboard, and mouse. Employees select their preferred laptop at hire from three options: MacBook Pro 14-inch (Apple silicon), ThinkPad X1 Carbon, or Dell XPS 15. Peripherals are refreshed on a four-year cycle. Additional equipment requests are reviewed by IT with manager approval. Personal use of company equipment is permitted in moderation, provided it does not introduce security risk. Software installed outside of the IT-approved catalog requires a security review, completed by submitting a request through the IT portal. Unapproved software discovered on company devices is removed without notice. Loss or damage must be reported to the IT Help Desk within 24 hours. Leave Request Procedure PTO and sick leave are requested through the HR portal under the Time Off section. Immediate managers are notified automatically upon submission. For planned PTO requests longer than 5 consecutive days, the manager has 5 business days to approve or request a schedule change; requests are not automatically denied. Parental leave, sabbatical, bereavement leave, medical leave, and military leave are initiated by contacting the HR Business Partner directly, who provides the required forms and coordinates the leave calendar. All leave requests involving a health condition are handled under strict confidentiality by HR; managers receive only the start date, expected return date, and whether the leave is approved. Employees should mark their Apex calendar and Slack status for any absence longer than one business day so that colleagues have visibility for scheduling purposes. Unpaid Leave Unpaid personal leave of up to 30 days may be granted at manager discretion after all accrued paid leave has been exhausted. Requests for unpaid leave must be submitted to the HR Business Partner with a brief explanation of the need. Benefits, including medical, dental, and vision, continue during approved unpaid leave, with the employee responsible for the standard employee-share premium, which may be billed directly during the leave period. Equity vesting continues during unpaid leave of up to 30 days; longer unpaid leaves may pause vesting. Medical leave under the FMLA or state family leave laws follows applicable statutory rules for continuation of benefits and job protection. Employees should contact their HR Business Partner as early as possible when a medical leave is anticipated. Questions about leave eligibility, accrual balances, or scheduling conflicts should be directed to the HR Business Partner for the employee's department. Accrual balances are also visible at any time in the ADP Self-Service portal.

Geographic Differentials Base salary is adjusted based on the employee's primary work location on the first day of each calendar year. Employees who relocate must notify HR within 30 days; the new geographic differential applies to their compensation at the next pay cycle after notification. Tier 1 locations, set at 100% of the published band, include: San Francisco Bay Area, New York City metropolitan area, and Seattle metropolitan area. Tier 2 locations, set at 95% of the published band, include: Boston, Washington DC metropolitan area, Los Angeles, San Diego, Chicago, Austin, Denver, and Miami. Tier 3 locations, set at 90% of the published band, cover all other U.S. locations including fully remote employees outside Tier 1 and Tier 2 cities. Employees in Tier 3 cities still participate in the same level framework and bonus and equity programs. International employees are compensated using local market benchmarks and are not subject to the U.S. geographic differential table. Variable Pay — Performance Bonus Non-sales, non-executive employees at L1 through L5 are eligible for an annual performance bonus with an on-target value of 10% of base salary. L6 Directors have a target bonus of 20%. L7 Vice Presidents have a target of 30%, as individually negotiated. Bonus payouts are calculated as the product of a company performance multiplier and an individual performance multiplier. The company multiplier ranges from 0 to 1.5 based on achievement of the annual plan; achievement below 70% of plan results in a 0 multiplier. The individual multiplier ranges from 0 to 1.5 based on the employee's September performance rating: exceptional earns 1.5, exceeds expectations earns 1.2, meets expectations earns 1.0, developing earns 0.5, and unsatisfactory earns 0. The two multipliers are applied to the target bonus amount, capping total bonus at 200% of target. Bonuses are paid in March of the following year. To be eligible, an employee must be active on February 28 of the payment year and must have been employed for at least six months of the preceding performance year. Employees who were on approved leave for part of the year receive a prorated bonus based on months worked. Sales Compensation Sales roles — Account Executive, Sales Development Representative, Sales Engineer, and Customer Success Manager — operate on individual sales compensation plans issued at the start of each fiscal year or at hire. Plans specify the base salary, on-target variable, quota, and accelerator and decelerator structure. Standard structures are either 50/50 base-to-variable for pure quota-carrying roles

PATCH /users/{user_id} updates a user's role or active status. DELETE /users/{user_id} deactivates the user and revokes all active sessions; the user record is retained for audit purposes. Role management: GET /roles returns the built-in roles (administrator, analyst, viewer) and all customer-defined roles. POST /roles creates a custom role with a name, a description, and an array of permission strings. PATCH /roles/{role_id} updates the permission set of a custom role; the change propagates to all active sessions within 60 seconds via the OPA policy bundle refresh cycle. API key management: POST /api-keys issues a new API key for a service account. The request body includes a name, a role, and an optional expiry date. The response includes the key_id and the plaintext secret in a single_use_secret field. The plaintext secret is shown exactly once and is not stored by Apex. GET /api-keys lists all active keys with their names, roles, creation dates, and last-used timestamps. DELETE /api-keys/{key_id} immediately revokes the key. Tenant configuration: GET /tenant returns the current tenant configuration, including the identity provider settings, rate limit tiers, data retention policy, and feature flags enabled for the tenant. PATCH /tenant updates mutable configuration fields. Multi-region replication settings are not mutable through the API; contact support for changes. Webhooks Apex delivers event notifications to customer-configured HTTP endpoints. Supported event types are: ingestion.completed, ingestion.failed, schema.changed, query.completed (for async jobs), audit.event, and billing.threshold_reached. Webhook subscriptions are created with POST /webhooks. The request body specifies the endpoint URL (HTTPS only), an array of event types to subscribe to, and a human-readable description. The response includes the webhook_id and a webhook_secret used to verify payloads. Every webhook request includes the headers X-Apex-Webhook-Id, X-Apex-Event-Type, X-Apex-Delivery-Id, and X-Apex-Timestamp (Unix epoch seconds, UTC). Payloads are signed with HMAC-SHA256 using the webhook secret. The signature is in the X-Apex-Signature-256 header and is computed over the raw request body. Receiver implementations must verify the signature before processing the payload and must reject requests where the X-Apex-Timestamp is more than 5 minutes in the past to prevent replay attacks. Apex delivers webhook events with at-least-once semantics. Receivers must be idempotent; the X-Apex-Delivery-Id header provides a stable identifier per event occurrence that can be used for deduplication. Apex retries delivery up to 5 times with exponential backoff (1, 5, 25, 125, 625 seconds) if the receiver returns any non-2xx response. After 5 failures, the event is written to the webhook dead-letter log accessible through GET /webhooks/{webhook_id}/failures. Pagination All list endpoints use cursor-based pagination. When a response contains more results than the requested page_size, the response body includes a next_cursor field containing an opaque string. Pass this value as the cursor query parameter in the next request. The default page size is 25; the maximum is 200. Cursor values are valid for 24 hours; requests with an expired cursor receive HTTP 400 with a cursor_expired type. Offset-based pagination is not supported, as it produces inconsistent results on

For example, if an employee is already traveling to a personal vacation destination and adds a customer meeting, only the transportation to the customer meeting from the vacation location is reimbursable, not the cost of the trip itself. Corporate Card Program Employees in roles that involve travel more than twice per quarter are eligible for an Apex corporate card issued through Navan. Cards are suspended automatically for repeated late reconciliation. Card privileges are revoked on the last day of employment. Exceptions and Dispute Resolution Requests for exceptions to these guidelines must be submitted in Navan as a pre-approval request, ideally before the expense is incurred. The finance business partner reviews exception requests within 3 business days. Exceptions that are outside the finance business partner's authority are escalated to the VP of Finance. Denied expense reimbursements may be appealed in writing to the VP of Finance within 10 business days of the denial. Apex Systems Code of Conduct Effective January 1, 2026 This Code of Conduct sets out the standards of professional behavior expected of every Apex Systems employee, contractor, and temporary worker. It applies to all work-related activities regardless of location, including in-office work, remote work, travel, off-site events, and interactions conducted through digital channels. Apex Systems expects everyone to read this document, understand its contents, and uphold its standards in every professional interaction. Our Commitment to a Respectful Workplace Apex Systems is committed to maintaining a work environment that is professional, inclusive, and free from harassment, discrimination, and intimidation. Every person who works at or with Apex Systems deserves to be treated with dignity and respect. This commitment extends to relationships with colleagues, direct reports, managers, customers, vendors, partners, and anyone else encountered in a professional capacity. Professionalism and Workplace Behavior Employees are expected to conduct themselves with integrity and professionalism at all times. This includes arriving prepared and on time to meetings, communicating clearly and respectfully in writing and in person, honoring commitments to teammates and stakeholders, and maintaining confidentiality about sensitive business and personnel information. Disagreements, debates, and competing ideas are a normal and valued part of work at Apex Systems. Employees are encouraged to challenge ideas constructively, offer alternative perspectives, and advocate for their positions. However, disagreement must remain focused on ideas and work products, not on individuals. Personal attacks, condescension, dismissiveness, or hostile communication are not acceptable in any context. Employees should avoid language or behavior in the workplace that a reasonable person would find offensive, demeaning, or exclusionary. This includes offensive jokes, slurs, stereotyping, deliberate use of incorrect pronouns, and comments about physical appearance, age, religion, national origin, or other personal characteristics. Such behavior will be treated as a conduct violation regardless of the intent behind it. Anti-Harassment Policy Apex Systems prohibits harassment of any kind in the workplace. Harassment is unwanted conduct that is severe or pervasive enough to create a hostile, abusive, or offensive work environment, or that results in an adverse employment decision. Harassment may be verbal, written, physical, or visual in nature. Sexual harassment is a specific and serious form of prohibited conduct. It includes, but is not limited to: unwanted sexual advances, requests for sexual favors, sexually explicit or suggestive comments, sharing sexually explicit images or materials in the workplace, and any conduct of a sexual nature that is unwanted by the recipient. Sexual harassment by a person in a position of authority over another employee is treated with particular seriousness given the power imbalance involved. Other forms of prohibited harassment include conduct targeting an individual based on race, color, religion, national origin, sex, gender identity, sexual orientation, age, disability, genetic information, veteran status, or any other characteristic protected by applicable law. Harassment by customers, vendors, or third parties toward Apex Systems employees is also covered by this policy. Employees who are harassed by an external party should report the conduct to their manager or People Operations. A single isolated incident may constitute harassment if it is sufficiently severe. The FP&A team presents a forecast accuracy report showing how each quarterly reforecast compared to actual results, which cost centers had the most chronic variances, and where the planning calendar or template should be adjusted for the current year. Approval process: No budget amendments are issued following the Q4 and annual review, since the fiscal year is closed. Department heads whose full-year actuals deviated from plan by more than 15% on any single cost category are required to meet with the CFO and their finance business partner in February to discuss root causes and process improvements before the current-year planning cycle begins. Budget Structure and Coding Budgets are organized by cost center. Each cost center is a six-digit code assigned by Finance and corresponds to a single functional team with one designated owner and one finance business partner. Cost center owners are responsible for all spending within their center. Expenses are classified by natural account, which identifies the type of expense: 6100 — Personnel (salaries, bonus, benefits, payroll taxes) 6200 — Software and SaaS subscriptions 6300 — Professional services and consulting 6400 — Facilities and utilities 6500 — Marketing and demand generation 6600 — Travel and entertainment 6700 — Other operating expenses Every expense transaction committed in the accounting system must carry a cost center code, a natural account code, and, for project-tracked spend, a project code assigned by Finance. Transactions missing required codes are returned to the submitter; vendors are not paid until coding is complete. Capitalized Versus Operating Expense Classification Software developed for internal use must be assessed for capitalization eligibility under ASC 350-40. A project is eligible for capitalization when three conditions are met: the project has entered the application development stage (preliminary project activities, including evaluation and selection of alternatives, are complete), the project has a defined scope and estimated budget, and management has authorized and committed resources to completing the project. Costs eligible for capitalization include external consulting fees for development work, employee salaries for time directly attributable to development, and interest during development. Time-based one-time password authenticators are acceptable for other systems where hardware keys are not supported. SMS-based two-factor authentication is not permitted for any production system access. API keys and tokens are credentials and must be treated as such. They must be stored in the secrets manager. They must not be committed to code repositories under any circumstances, including test repositories and forks. If a credential is accidentally committed, it must be treated as compromised: rotate it immediately and report the exposure to the Security Engineering team. The git history must be cleaned using the approved remediation process. Secrets rotation is required on the following schedule: API keys for external services every ninety days, database credentials every thirty days, service account tokens every sixty days. Rotation must not cause service disruption; engineers must implement rotation in a backward-compatible manner using the secrets manager's versioning features. The Security team monitors for credentials not rotated within the required window and will raise a finding. SSH keys used for server access must be at least 4096-bit RSA or use Ed25519. Private keys must be passphrase-protected. Public keys are registered through the IAM system. Personal SSH keys must not be shared and must be rotated annually or immediately upon suspected compromise. Secure Development Practices Security must be considered from the design phase, not added at the end. Before beginning development on any feature that handles authentication, authorization, sensitive data, or external integrations, engineers must review the design with the Security Engineering team. For features classified as high-risk per the security risk classification matrix, a formal threat model must be completed before coding begins. Code review is a required gate before any code merges to the main branch. Every pull request must be reviewed by at least one engineer who is not the author. Pull requests that touch authentication logic, authorization checks, cryptography, session management, data access controls, input validation, or external API integrations require review by a member of the Security Engineering team or a designated security champion on the team. Security Code Review Checklist: Reviewers must verify the following for all pull requests: Input validation: All data entering the system from external sources — user input, API calls, file uploads, webhooks — must be validated and sanitized. Validation should fail closed: reject anything that does not conform to the expected format, type, and range. Authentication and authorization checks: Every endpoint and function that accesses sensitive data or performs privileged operations must verify that the requester is authenticated and authorized. Authorization checks must be performed server-side. Client-side enforcement alone is not acceptable. SQL and query injection: Parameterized queries or prepared statements must be used for all database queries. String concatenation for query construction is prohibited. Output encoding: Data rendered in HTML must be encoded to prevent cross-site scripting. Use framework-provided escaping functions. Do not construct HTML strings by concatenation. Cryptography: Do not implement custom cryptographic algorithms. Use vetted libraries. AES-256 is required for symmetric

HDHP with HSA versus PPO: Choosing the Right Plan The right plan depends on your expected healthcare usage, your financial situation, and your tolerance for variable out-of-pocket costs. The HDHP is recommended for employees who are generally healthy and anticipate low to moderate medical utilization in the coming year. HDHP premiums are approximately 40% lower than PPO premiums — an employee-only enrollee pays $30 per month on the HDHP versus $85 on the PPO, a saving of $660 per year. Add the $500 employer HSA contribution, and a healthy employee who incurs minimal medical expenses in a given year comes out meaningfully ahead on the HDHP even before making any personal HSA contributions. Employees who are building toward long-term financial security benefit from the HSA's ability to accumulate and invest balances over time. The PPO tends to be the better choice for employees with predictable, significant healthcare needs. If you or a covered dependent regularly sees specialists, takes ongoing brand-name medications, or anticipates a planned procedure, the PPO's lower deductible ($500 individual versus $1,500), fixed copays, and richer prescription coverage reduce out-of-pocket exposure for those known expenses. The PPO also has an embedded individual deductible structure for family plans, which provides more predictability when only one family member has high utilization. To compare the two plans, consider your prior year's healthcare spending and estimate your likely costs for the coming year. Calculate total annual cost under each plan as: (monthly premium contribution times twelve) plus expected out-of-pocket medical and prescription costs. For many employees who are healthy, the HDHP total will be lower even after accounting for the higher deductible, particularly when the employer HSA contribution offsets initial out-of-pocket expenses. People Operations is available to assist with plan comparison questions during open enrollment. passing required status checks, no unresolved review comments marked as blocking, and no merge conflicts. Force-pushing to open PRs is disallowed. Approved PRs are merged using the squash-and-merge strategy to keep the main branch history linear. For changes that affect the API contract, the Helm values schema, the Kafka message schema, or the database migration files, a second approval from the platform-owners team is required in addition to the CODEOWNERS approval. Continuous Integration Pipeline CI runs on GitHub Actions using self-hosted runners on c6i.4xlarge EC2 instances provisioned by the runner manager. Self-hosted runners avoid the memory and disk limits of GitHub-hosted runners and allow Bazel's remote cache to be used without network latency. The standard pipeline is triggered on every push to a pull request branch and on every merge to main. The pipeline stages run in order: Stage 1 — Build: Bazel builds all affected targets. The build cache hit rate is monitored; a cache miss rate above 15% triggers a review of the cache key configuration. Stage 2 — Unit tests: Bazel runs all unit tests affected by the changed targets. Tests must complete in under 5 minutes per service; tests that exceed this threshold are flagged for splitting or optimization. Stage 3 — Integration tests: Integration tests run against ephemeral Kind clusters provisioned by the test framework. Each Kind cluster is bootstrapped with the current chart versions and the changed service revision. Integration tests cover the happy path and documented error paths for each service API. Stage 4 — Static analysis: golangci-lint for Go services, ruff for Python, clippy for Rust, eslint for TypeScript. Analysis failures are treated as build failures. Stage 5 — Security scans: Semgrep runs a ruleset covering OWASP Top 10 and Apex custom rules for common misconfigurations. Trivy scans every container image for known CVEs with critical or high severity; images with unfixed critical CVEs are blocked from promotion. The SBOM is generated for every image and stored in the internal artifact registry. Total pipeline duration must remain under 25 minutes. The DevEx team monitors pipeline duration weekly and works with teams to optimize slow stages. Continuous Delivery and Progressive Delivery Merges to main trigger the delivery pipeline via Argo CD. The pipeline is defined as an Argo Workflow that orchestrates build, sign, publish, and deploy stages. Builds are triggered by a push event from GitHub. The container image is built by Kaniko running inside the cluster, avoiding the need for Docker daemon access. The image is signed with cosign using a key stored in AWS KMS. The signed image is pushed to the internal ECR registry. Image signing is verified at deployment time by Kyverno policies; images without a valid signature are rejected by the admission controller. Deployment to staging is fully automatic on every merge to main. The Helm chart for the changed service is updated with the new image tag, committed to the gitops repository, and synced by Argo CD within two minutes. Deployment to canary is gated by a human approval

It applies to all regular full-time employees based in the United States. Employees working outside the United States should consult the local addendum maintained by the HR Business Partner for their region, which may provide different or additional entitlements required by local law. Paid Time Off Full-time employees accrue PTO at the rate of 1.67 days per calendar month, for a total of 20 days per year. Accrual begins on the first day of employment and is credited at the start of each calendar month. Part-time employees working at least 20 hours per week accrue PTO on a prorated basis. PTO encompasses vacation, personal days, and any other discretionary time away from work that does not fall into one of the specific categories below. Employees are encouraged to use their PTO throughout the year to maintain work-life balance. Managers are responsible for proactively planning coverage to ensure employees can take leave without jeopardizing customer commitments. PTO may be taken in minimum increments of half a day. Requests of less than three consecutive days require at least three business days of advance notice. Requests of three or more consecutive days require manager approval submitted through the HR portal at least two weeks before the intended start date. Up to five unused PTO days may be carried over into the following calendar year. Any accrual above the five-day carryover cap is forfeited on December 31. Employees who were unable to use PTO due to business-critical needs may apply to their HR Business Partner for an exception carryover of up to five additional days, subject to VP approval. Employees leaving the company are paid out for all accrued but unused PTO in their final paycheck, subject to the laws of the employee's home state. California employees are paid out at 100% regardless of any forfeiture provisions above; other states vary. Sick Leave Sick leave is provided separately from PTO at 10 days per year, front-loaded on January 1. New hires receive a prorated sick leave balance on their start date based on the number of months remaining in the calendar year. Sick leave may be used for: personal illness or injury, medical or dental appointments, mental health days, preventive care, and care for an immediate family member who is ill, injured, or attending a medical appointment. Absences of three consecutive days or more require a note from a licensed healthcare provider confirming the employee's inability to work and the anticipated return date. The note does not need to include a diagnosis. Apex does not require medical notes for absences of one or two days. Unused sick leave does not carry over from year to year and is not paid out on separation. Company Holidays Apex observes 11 paid holidays each calendar year. Employees working in the United States receive: New Year's Day (January 1), Martin Luther King Jr. Day (third Monday in January), Presidents' Day (third Monday in February), Memorial Day (last Monday in May), Juneteenth (June 19), Independence Day (July 4), Labor Day (first Monday in September), Thanksgiving Day (fourth Thursday in November), the day after Thanksgiving (Friday), Christmas Eve (December 24), and Christmas Day (December 25). Employees required to work on a company holiday because of customer coverage, on-call rotation, or operational necessity receive a compensatory day off to be taken within the same quarter. Apex Systems Audit Procedures Effective January 1, 2026 This document describes the internal audit function, audit committee governance, control testing methodology, findings management, and the relationship with the external auditor at Apex Systems. It is intended for the Internal Audit team, the Audit Committee, the Chief Financial Officer, department heads who own controls under audit, and the external audit engagement team. The goal of the audit function at Apex Systems is to provide independent assurance that internal controls are operating effectively, that financial reporting is accurate, and that regulatory and policy compliance is maintained. Internal Audit Function Apex Systems maintains an Internal Audit function that reports directly to the Audit Committee of the Board of Directors. This reporting structure is intentional and essential to the independence of the function. While the Chief Financial Officer provides day-to-day administrative support to the Internal Audit team, the Audit Committee hires, evaluates, and terminates the Head of Internal Audit. The Audit Committee approves the annual internal audit plan and receives all audit findings. The Internal Audit team has unrestricted access to all financial records, systems, personnel, and physical locations required to perform its work. Management may not restrict access to audit evidence or information unless the restriction is approved in advance by the Audit Committee Chair. Any attempt to impede, limit, or influence audit work must be reported by the Head of Internal Audit to the Audit Committee immediately. The Internal Audit team consists of the Head of Internal Audit, who holds a Certified Internal Auditor designation, and a team of staff auditors. Staff auditors hold or are pursuing professional certifications appropriate to their area of specialization. The team is staffed to complete the annual audit plan with no more than fifteen percent reliance on co-sourced resources from an external firm. When co-sourcing is used, the selected firm must not be the same firm serving as the external auditor to preserve independence. Audit Committee Charter The Audit Committee of the Board of Directors is established by and operates pursuant to the Audit Committee Charter adopted by the full Board. The Charter is reviewed annually and updated as needed. The key provisions of the Charter are summarized here; the full Charter is maintained in the board governance system. Composition: The Audit Committee consists of at least three independent directors. Each member must meet the independence standards required by applicable securities laws and exchange listing standards, as if Apex Systems were a public company, in anticipation of a future public offering. At least one member must qualify as a financial expert, defined as a person with professional experience in public accounting, financial reporting under GAAP, or oversight of financial reporting at a public or large private company. The full Board appoints Audit Committee members annually. Meetings: The Audit Committee meets at least quarterly. Meetings include time with the Head of Internal Audit and the lead external audit partner without management present. The CFO and Controller attend Audit Committee meetings by invitation and present materials on financial reporting and control matters. Meeting minutes are taken and approved at the

Apex Systems Public API — Developer Documentation API version v2 — Revision January 2026 The Apex Systems Public API enables customers to programmatically ingest data, execute analytical queries, manage users and roles, configure webhooks, and monitor audit events. This document is the primary reference for developers integrating with the v2 API. The full machine-readable specification is published as an OpenAPI 3.1 document at https://api.apex-systems.example/openapi.json and is kept in sync with each release. Base URL and Versioning The v2 API is served at https://api.apex-systems.example/v2. All HTTPS requests must use TLS 1.2 or higher. TLS 1.0 and 1.1 connections are rejected at the load balancer. Apex uses semantic versioning for the API. Breaking changes — removal of a field, change to an existing field's type or constraints, change in HTTP status code semantics, removal of an endpoint — always increment the major version. A deprecated major version is maintained for a minimum of 12 months after the successor version reaches general availability, after which it is sunset with 90 days of advance notice via the developer changelog and a deprecation header added to responses. Non-breaking additions — new optional request fields, new response fields, new optional query parameters, new endpoints — may appear in the current version at any time. Clients must be written to tolerate unknown fields in responses. The current API version is included in every response in the X-Apex-Api-Version header. If a client detects that the version in the response differs from the expected version, it should log a warning and test for forward compatibility. Authentication Every API request must include a bearer token in the Authorization HTTP header: Authorization: Bearer {token}. Two token types are supported. API keys are long-lived credentials associated with a service account. They are issued from the Apex administrator console, scoped to one or more roles at issuance time, and remain valid until explicitly revoked. API keys are the recommended authentication method for server-to-server integrations. The key secret is shown exactly once at creation and is not recoverable; if lost, a replacement must be issued and the old key revoked. API keys do not expire automatically but should be rotated on a 90-day schedule as a security best practice. OAuth 2.0 access tokens are obtained through the authorization code flow with PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange), the recommended flow for interactive applications where the user authenticates with their identity provider. The Apex OIDC discovery document is published at https://auth.apex-systems.example/.well-known/openid-configuration. Access tokens are short-lived with a 15-minute expiry. Refresh tokens are issued alongside access tokens and are valid for 30 days, rotated on each use. Unauthenticated requests receive HTTP 401 Unauthorized with a WWW-Authenticate header. Authenticated requests that lack the required role for the requested operation receive HTTP 403 Forbidden. The error response body follows the standard error model described below. Rate Limits The API enforces rate limits at the tenant level. Default limits apply to all tenants unless overridden by a plan-level or contract-level configuration. Default limits: 600 requests per minute across all API keys and access tokens combined, with a burst allowance of 60

contract terms, and Procurement team approval. Payment Terms Apex Systems' standard payment terms are net thirty days from receipt of a valid invoice. The Procurement team negotiates longer payment terms (net forty-five or net sixty) for large contracts where it is commercially reasonable to do so. Early payment discounts (e.g., two percent ten net thirty) may be accepted at the discretion of the CFO when the cash position supports it. Invoices must reference the relevant purchase order number. The Procurement team maintains purchase orders for all approved contracts. Invoices without a matching purchase order will not be processed by Accounts Payable until a purchase order is issued or the Procurement team provides written authorization. The Accounts Payable team processes payments on the Friday of each week. Emergency payments outside this cycle require CFO approval. Payments to new vendors require vendor setup in the ERP system, including verification of banking details through the bank confirmation process. The bank confirmation process requires direct outreach to the vendor using contact information on file, not information provided in the payment instruction itself, to prevent fraud. Vendor Risk Management Vendors who provide critical services or who handle Apex Systems or customer data are subject to ongoing risk management. The Procurement and Security teams maintain a tiered vendor risk classification. Tier one vendors are those providing critical infrastructure, handling customer data, or providing services that would cause significant business disruption if unavailable. Tier two vendors provide important but non-critical services. Tier three vendors provide commodity or low-risk goods and services. Tier one vendors are assessed annually. The assessment includes a review of their security posture (SOC 2 Type II report review or equivalent), financial health, SLA performance against contract, and any changes in their business that could affect service quality or risk. Tier two vendors are assessed every two years. Tier three vendors are not subject to periodic assessment unless a risk event occurs. Results of vendor risk assessments are shared with the sponsoring department and escalated to the CFO for Tier one vendors with material findings. The Procurement team works with the sponsoring department to develop a remediation plan or contingency plan where risks are identified.

Step 3 — Initial Triage: The responding engineer performs an initial triage to assess the scope and severity of the issue. Triage should take no more than ten minutes. The engineer checks relevant dashboards in Grafana, reviews recent deployments in the deployment log, examines error rates and latency metrics, and reviews recent changes in infrastructure configuration. Based on the triage, the engineer assigns an initial severity level and opens an incident in PagerDuty. Step 4 — Incident Declaration: For P0 and P1 incidents, the responding engineer declares an official incident by posting a message in the #incidents Slack channel using the standard incident declaration template and creating an incident document in the shared incident log. The incident document is a running record of timeline, hypotheses, actions taken, and current status. Step 5 — Role Assignment: For P0 incidents, roles must be assigned within ten minutes of declaration. The Incident Commander owns the overall response, coordinates the team, makes decisions, and provides status updates. The Incident Commander does not do hands-on technical work. The Technical Lead owns the technical investigation and remediation. The Communications Lead owns internal and external communications including the status page. For P1 incidents, one person may hold multiple roles if team size requires it. Step 6 — Investigation and Mitigation: The technical lead and supporting engineers investigate root cause while simultaneously pursuing mitigations that reduce or eliminate customer impact. Mitigation takes priority over root cause analysis. Common mitigations include rolling back a recent deployment, disabling a feature flag, routing traffic away from a degraded service, increasing capacity, or failing over to a backup system. All actions taken during an incident must be logged in the incident document with a timestamp and the name of the engineer who took the action. Step 7 — Resolution: An incident is resolved when customer impact has been eliminated or reduced to an acceptable level and the system is stable. The incident commander declares resolution and posts a resolution message in #incidents. The status page is updated to reflect resolution. Post-resolution monitoring continues for at least one hour after resolution to confirm stability. Step 8 — Post-Incident Review: All P0 and P1 incidents require a postmortem. P2 incidents may require a postmortem at the discretion of the engineering manager. Escalation Process The on-call engineer should escalate proactively rather than waiting until it is too late. The following situations always require immediate escalation to the on-call engineering manager: any P0 incident, a P1 incident that has not been mitigated within two hours, any incident involving customer data exposure or security compromise, any incident requiring changes to production infrastructure that the on-call engineer is not authorized to make independently, or any incident where the on-call engineer is uncertain about the severity or impact. The on-call manager should escalate to the CTO and CEO for: any P0 incident (within fifteen minutes of declaration), any confirmed customer data breach, any incident that is likely

All audit evidence is documented in the working paper files and retained for seven years. Auditors record observations throughout fieldwork, including preliminary findings identified during testing. Reporting Phase: After fieldwork is complete, the audit team prepares a draft audit report. The report includes an executive summary, background on the audit scope, the overall audit opinion (defined below), and a description of each finding with a risk rating, root cause analysis, and a recommended management action. Draft reports are provided to the business area management team for management response. Management has ten business days to provide a written response to each finding that either accepts the finding and commits to a remediation action and timeline or disputes the finding with supporting evidence. The audit team considers management's response and may amend the finding if the evidence supports it. Final Report: The final audit report incorporates management responses and is issued to the Audit Committee, the CFO, and the relevant business area head. The Head of Internal Audit presents key findings to the Audit Committee at the next scheduled meeting. Control Testing Methodology Apex Systems uses a risk-based approach to control testing. Controls are classified as key controls or non-key controls. Key controls are those whose failure could result in a material misstatement in financial reporting, a significant operational disruption, or a regulatory compliance failure. Non-key controls are compensating or supplementary controls that reduce risk but are not individually critical. Key controls are tested at least annually. For automated controls implemented in systems, testing includes inspection of system configuration, review of change management records to confirm the control has not been modified, and end-to-end transaction testing. For manual controls, testing includes re-performance or observation of the control in operation and inspection of evidence produced by the control. Sample sizes for transactional testing follow a risk-based protocol. For controls that operate daily, the standard sample size is twenty-five items. For controls that operate weekly, the standard sample size is fifteen items. For controls that operate monthly, the standard sample is twelve items. For controls that operate quarterly, the full population is tested. Sample sizes may be increased if the initial sample produces exceptions. An exception is any test result that indicates a control failure. Every exception is documented and assessed for severity. A single exception in a manual control does not necessarily constitute a control deficiency; the auditor must assess whether the exception represents an isolated error or a systematic failure. Multiple exceptions in the same control indicate a deficiency and must be reported as a finding. Findings Classification All internal audit findings are assigned a risk rating that determines the urgency of remediation and the level of management attention required. Critical: A finding that indicates the absence or complete failure of a key control, presents an immediate risk of material financial misstatement, or indicates a material compliance violation. Critical findings are escalated immediately to the CFO and Audit Committee Chair and must have a remediation plan within fifteen business days. Critical findings that have not been remediated within sixty days are re-escalated to the

The OPA instance receives the JWT from the incoming request and evaluates the request against the current policy bundle, which includes three built-in roles (administrator, analyst, viewer) and any custom roles defined by the tenant. Policy bundles are distributed by a bundle server and refreshed every 60 seconds. JWTs issued to customer users are valid for 15 minutes. Refresh tokens are stored as HTTP-only cookies with a 30-day lifetime and are rotated on each use. API keys are long-lived bearer tokens scoped to one or more roles and revocable through the control plane API. Observability Every service emits structured JSON logs to standard output. The Fluent Bit DaemonSet on each node collects logs and ships them to Datadog, where they are indexed and searchable by service, tenant, severity, and correlation_id. Log retention is 90 days in Datadog. All logs containing customer data are tagged with the tenant_id and restricted to Apex staff with the data-access role in Datadog. Application metrics are collected by Prometheus and exposed on a standard /metrics endpoint. A regional Prometheus federation server aggregates metrics across all clusters and makes them available to Grafana dashboards and to the PagerDuty alerting integration. Alerts are tuned to the per-plane SLO targets to minimize noise. Distributed traces are instrumented with OpenTelemetry SDKs across all services. Traces are sampled at 10% for normal traffic and at 100% for requests that result in errors or exceed the 95th-percentile latency threshold. Trace data is shipped to Datadog APM. The trace context propagation header is W3C Trace Context, and all services are required to forward this header in outbound requests. Reliability Targets and Error Budgets The platform publishes formal SLOs in the customer-facing service agreement. The control plane targets 99.9% monthly availability, equivalent to 43.8 minutes of allowed downtime per month. The query plane targets 99.95% monthly availability, equivalent to 21.9 minutes per month. Availability is measured as the percentage of successful synthetic probes issued every 30 seconds from an external monitoring service. Error budgets are calculated monthly and reviewed at the reliability review on the first Wednesday of each month. When a plane's error budget is more than 50% consumed before the end of the month, non-critical deployments for that plane are frozen until the budget is restored or the month resets. When the budget is fully consumed, all deployments to the affected plane require VP of Engineering approval. Data Isolation and Tenant Boundaries Tenant data is strictly isolated at every layer. In the storage plane, each tenant's data resides in a dedicated S3 prefix within a shared bucket, with bucket policy conditions that reject cross-prefix access. In the query plane, Trino session properties are populated from the validated JWT and enforce row-level security policies that restrict each query to the requesting tenant's data. Cross-tenant queries are rejected at the planner level before any execution begins. The control plane performs a double authorization check: the OPA sidecar evaluates the tenant claim in the JWT, and the application layer independently validates that the requested resource belongs to the same tenant. The on-call schedule is published in PagerDuty at least two weeks in advance and managed by the Engineering Operations team. Primary On-Call Engineer: The primary on-call engineer is the first responder for all production alerts. They must acknowledge PagerDuty pages within five minutes. Secondary On-Call Engineer: The secondary on-call engineer serves as backup to the primary. They should be available to join an incident within ten minutes of being paged. The secondary should also monitor the primary's response to P0 and P1 incidents and be prepared to assist without being explicitly asked. On-Call Engineering Manager: An engineering manager is on call alongside the primary and secondary engineers. The on-call manager is paged automatically for all P0 incidents and must be manually paged by the incident commander for P1 incidents. The on-call manager is responsible for executive escalation, resource coordination, and approving decisions that require management authority. On-Call Handoff: At the end of each on-call shift, the outgoing engineer completes a written handoff in the designated Slack channel. The handoff includes a summary of any active incidents, any ongoing investigations, recent deployments, known system instabilities, and any context that the incoming engineer needs to start their shift informed. Handoffs are required even if the shift was uneventful. On-Call Compensation: Engineers receive on-call compensation as described in the engineering compensation supplement. Acknowledgment signals that a human is aware of the alert and actively investigating. Failure to acknowledge triggers escalation to the secondary. Step 3 — Initial Triage: The responding engineer performs an initial triage to assess the scope and severity of the issue. Triage should take no more than ten minutes. a customer-managed KMS key, and a processing job is enqueued. Streaming events are delivered through a webhook endpoint that writes messages to a per-tenant Kafka partition in near real time. The ingestion workers, written in Rust, consume from Kafka topics and process records in parallel. Each worker validates records against the tenant-defined schema stored in the schema registry, applies PII detection rules configured per tenant, strips or masks sensitive fields, and serializes clean records as Parquet files. Validated records are published to the normalized Kafka topic. Records that fail schema validation or exceed the size limit are routed to a tenant-specific dead-letter queue with a 14-day retention, after which they are deleted. DLQ events are surfaced in the customer console with a summary of the failure reason. Schema changes are applied through a versioned migration API rather than in-place modification. Each schema version is immutable once published. The ingestion worker uses the schema version embedded in each incoming message to route to the correct validation and serialization logic, allowing old and new versions to coexist during migration windows. Storage Plane Normalized records from the ingestion plane land in an Apache Iceberg data lake stored on S3. Iceberg provides ACID transaction guarantees, partition evolution, and time-travel queries. The Iceberg catalog is managed by a Go service backed by AWS Glue, which tracks table definitions, partition metadata, schema history, and compaction state. The catalog service exposes a gRPC API consumed by both the ingestion workers (for writing) and the Trino query engine (for reading). Hot partitions — those representing the most recent 48 hours of data for any tenant — are cached in Redis using a time-to-live keyed to the partition date. Dashboard tile queries that aggregate over recent partitions are satisfied from cache without hitting S3. The cache invalidation strategy is event-driven: the ingestion worker publishes a cache invalidation message to Kafka after each partition write, and the cache service consumes this to evict or refresh the affected keys. Summary aggregates for common dashboard metrics are precomputed by a scheduled Spark job running every 15 minutes and written to TimescaleDB. TimescaleDB is a PostgreSQL extension optimized for time-series data, and it serves the dashboard backend directly with sub-100-millisecond responses for tenant-scoped aggregate queries. Backups of the Iceberg catalog metadata and the TimescaleDB summaries run hourly using AWS Backup. Catalog metadata is replicated cross-region with a recovery point objective of 15 minutes. Full S3 object-level replication provides an additional layer of data durability. The recovery time objective for a full storage plane failure is 30 minutes for metadata and 4 hours for full data availability. Query Plane Customer analytical queries are executed by Trino, an open-source distributed SQL engine. Each pricing tier — Standard, Professional, and Enterprise — runs a dedicated Trino cluster to provide performance isolation. Enterprise cluster nodes are provisioned at r6i.xlarge, allowing larger working sets for complex joins and window functions. Query planning is enhanced by a cost-based optimizer that is calibrated nightly. The calibration job samples recent query history, measures actual execution statistics, and updates table statistics stored in the

High Deductible Health Plan The HDHP is a lower-premium plan with higher deductibles that qualifies as an HSA-eligible health plan under IRS rules. Because the HDHP carries significantly lower monthly premiums than the PPO, it is designed to be paired with a Health Savings Account. Employees who are generally healthy, have low expected medical utilization, or who want to build tax-advantaged savings for future healthcare costs often find the HDHP to be the more financially efficient choice when total annual cost — premiums plus out-of-pocket spending — is considered. Deductibles and Out-of-Pocket Maximums The HDHP has a higher deductible than the PPO, meaning employees pay the full cost of most services until the deductible is met. The in-network deductible is $1,500 per individual and $3,000 per family. For family coverage, the family deductible is an aggregate deductible: any combination of family members' expenses counts toward the single $3,000 threshold, and the plan begins paying once that combined total is reached. This differs from plans with embedded individual deductibles, where each family member must satisfy their own deductible independently. The in-network out-of-pocket maximum is $4,000 per individual and $8,000 per family. This is the most any covered individual or family will pay in a plan year for in-network covered services. After the out-of-pocket maximum is reached, the plan pays one hundred percent of covered in-network services for the remainder of the plan year. Out-of-pocket accumulation includes deductible payments, coinsurance, and copays, but not premiums. Preventive care is covered at one hundred percent before the deductible under the HDHP, consistent with ACA requirements. This means annual physicals, immunizations, and cancer screenings do not require the deductible to be met first. All other covered services — including office visits, specialist visits, diagnostic tests, and most prescriptions — apply to the deductible before the plan contributes. In-Network Cost-Sharing After Deductible Once the deductible is satisfied, all covered in-network services are paid at eighty percent by the plan and twenty percent by the employee (coinsurance), until the out-of-pocket maximum is reached. The HDHP does not use fixed copays for office visits or specialist visits. Every service is subject to coinsurance only. Emergency room visits are covered at eighty percent coinsurance after the deductible. Inpatient hospital stays and outpatient surgery are also covered at eighty percent coinsurance after the deductible. Prescription Drug Coverage Under the HDHP All prescriptions are subject to the deductible — there are no pre-deductible copays for medications, with the exception of preventive medications designated by the carrier. After the deductible is met, cost-sharing mirrors the PPO: Tier 1 generic drugs are $10 per thirty-day supply, Tier 2 preferred brand drugs are $35, Tier 3 non-preferred brand drugs are $70, and specialty drugs are subject to twenty percent coinsurance with a $150 per-prescription maximum. (ARR), defined as the annualized value of all active subscription contracts as of the measurement date, excluding one-time fees and services. Net new ARR, the change in ARR from one period to the next. Gross dollar retention (GDR), the percentage of beginning ARR retained from existing customers excluding expansions, measured annually. Net dollar retention (NDR), the percentage of beginning ARR retained including expansions and contractions from existing customers, measured annually. Customer acquisition cost (CAC), total sales and marketing expense divided by the number of new customers acquired. Magic number, the net new ARR generated per dollar of sales and marketing spend in the prior period. Rule of 40, the sum of the trailing-12-month revenue growth rate and the EBITDA margin, indicating efficiency. Free cash flow is defined as cash from operations minus capital expenditures, including capitalized internal-use software development costs. Burn multiple is the net cash burned per dollar of net new ARR added, a measure of capital efficiency. Board Reporting The monthly board flash report is distributed on the eighth business day after month end. The flash report covers: month-end ARR and net new ARR versus plan, cash and ending runway at the current burn rate, headcount and open positions, key sales metrics including pipeline and bookings, and one or two commentary bullets on notable operational developments. The flash report is prepared by FP&A and reviewed by the CFO before distribution. The quarterly board management report is distributed at least five business days before the board meeting. It includes: the audited or reviewed financial statements, the CEO memo covering strategic priorities and key decisions, the FP&A operating review with variance commentary, the revenue operations report, and the engineering and product operations summary. Board members submit questions to the CFO at least 48 hours before the meeting to allow for preparation. Internal Controls Apex operates a structured internal control framework informed by the COSO framework and sized appropriately for a pre-IPO software company. The audit committee reviews the control framework annually and receives a control effectiveness report from the internal audit function. Segregation of duties: No single employee may initiate, approve, and record a financial transaction. Approval authority in the accounting system is enforced at the role level; engineers and support staff do not have access to financial systems. Journal entries require a preparer and a separate approver. Manual journal entries above $25,000 require the Controller's approval. Journal entry controls: all journal entries are logged with a preparer ID, a timestamp, and a supporting document reference. A management report of all manual journal entries is produced monthly and reviewed by the Controller. Recurring journal entries are documented in a recurring entry schedule and approved quarterly. Reconciliation controls: every balance sheet account is reconciled monthly to a subledger or external source. Reconciliations are signed off by the preparer and a second reviewer. Parental Leave Apex provides 16 weeks of fully paid parental leave for the primary caregiver and 8 weeks of fully paid parental leave for the secondary caregiver. During parental leave, Apex continues all benefits at the normal employee premium contribution rate. Parental leave runs concurrently with any federal Family and Medical Leave Act entitlements. Employees who have worked at Apex for fewer than 12 months or fewer than 1,250 hours in the preceding 12 months are not eligible for FMLA protection but remain eligible for paid parental leave. The returning employee has the right to the same position or an equivalent position with the same base pay, bonus opportunity, and equity. Apex does not permit role changes, team transfers, or reporting structure changes initiated by the company during an employee's parental leave without the employee's written consent. Employees receive up to 1 paid day for the loss of a close friend or colleague at the manager's discretion. Bereavement leave may be taken non-consecutively within 30 days of the death to accommodate travel, services, or estate obligations. Anti-Discrimination Policy Employment decisions at Apex Systems are made on the basis of merit, qualifications, and business need. Employees who believe they have been subjected to discriminatory treatment in an employment decision should report the concern to People Operations. Conflicts of Interest A conflict of interest arises when an employee's personal interests, financial interests, or outside relationships could influence or appear to influence their judgment or actions on behalf of Apex Systems. Employees are expected to make decisions solely in the best interest of Apex Systems and must disclose any situation that could create a conflict. Common examples of conflicts of interest include: having a financial stake in a vendor, customer, or competitor; hiring or supervising a close family member or romantic partner; accepting gifts or entertainment from vendors or business partners beyond the limits described in this policy; working for or consulting with a competitor, vendor, or customer while employed at Apex Systems; and using Apex Systems resources, information, or relationships for personal gain. The company does not prohibit all outside activities. However, employees must disclose any outside employment, board membership, advisory role, or financial interest that could reasonably create or appear to create a conflict of interest. Disclosures are submitted to People Operations and reviewed by the Chief People Officer and General Counsel. The company may require an employee to modify the outside activity or disengage from it if a material conflict is found. Gift and Entertainment Policy Employees may not accept or provide gifts, meals, entertainment, or travel with a value exceeding one hundred fifty dollars from or to any vendor, customer, or business partner. Gifts of cash or cash equivalents including gift cards are never permitted regardless of amount. Gifts must not be accepted or given in connection with a pending or anticipated business decision. Employees who receive unsolicited gifts that exceed the limit must return them or surrender them to People Operations. Entertainment including meals and events is permitted at a reasonable level when there is a legitimate business purpose and the cost is not lavish. Quarterly Budget Review Schedule Apex Systems conducts four formal quarterly budget reviews each year. These reviews are distinct from the monthly finance leadership review and the twice-yearly reforecast process. The quarterly reviews are the primary forum where department heads and the CFO assess performance against plan, align on a revised full-year outlook, and make formal decisions about budget adjustments. Each review follows a fixed calendar, a defined attendee list, a structured agenda, and a documented approval process. Q1 Budget Review — April The Q1 review is held during the first two weeks of April, covering actual results for January through March. The review is a half-day session, typically three hours, scheduled by the FP&A team no later than March 25. Attendees: CFO, VP Finance, FP&A lead, all finance business partners, and all department heads. The CEO attends the opening segment covering company-level revenue performance and exits before the cost center breakout sessions. Legal and People Operations department heads attend only their respective cost center segment. What is reviewed: Actuals versus budget for Q1 across all cost centers and natural account categories. Variance analysis for every line item exceeding the materiality threshold of 10% or $25,000, whichever is greater. Personnel cost actuals versus headcount plan, including any open requisitions, recent hires, and attrition. Software and SaaS spend versus contracted commitments. A Q1 cash flow summary and comparison to the cash forecast approved in December. Early signals on full-year revenue tracking. The FP&A team presents a preliminary Q1 reforecast for the full year based on Q1 actuals and any known changes to business conditions. Approval process: Within five business days of the Q1 review, department heads submit any budget amendment requests identified during the session to their finance business partner. The FP&A team consolidates requests and presents a recommended amendment package to the CFO within ten business days. The CFO approves, rejects, or defers each amendment. the following year's budget guidance. Attendees: CFO, VP Finance, FP&A lead, all finance business partners, and all department heads. submission_id (UUID), a submitted_count, a rejected_count, and an array of rejection details. The submission is processed asynchronously; the submission status can be polled using GET /submissions/{submission_id}. POST /datasets/{dataset_id}/files Creates a pre-signed S3 PUT URL for uploading a file directly to Apex storage without passing through the API servers. Accepted file formats are Parquet, CSV (UTF-8 with configurable delimiter), and newline-delimited JSON. Maximum file size is 5 GB. The request body specifies the filename, format, and expected row count (optional, used for progress reporting). The response contains the pre-signed URL (valid for 2 hours), an upload_id, and the expected Content-Type header to use in the PUT request. After the file is uploaded to the pre-signed URL, the file must be registered by calling POST /datasets/{dataset_id}/files/{upload_id}/complete. This triggers ingestion processing. Progress can be monitored through GET /datasets/{dataset_id}/files/{upload_id}/status. GET /submissions/{submission_id} Returns the current status of a batch ingestion submission. Possible statuses are pending, processing, complete, and failed. A complete response includes the total record count, the accepted count, and the rejected count. A failed response includes a top-level error describing the systemic failure. Schema management: GET /datasets/{dataset_id}/schema returns the current schema version, column definitions, and validation rules. POST /datasets/{dataset_id}/schema creates a new schema version. Schema versions are immutable once a dataset has data written under them; older versions continue to support ingestion until explicitly deprecated by the dataset owner. Query Endpoints POST /datasets/{dataset_id}/query Executes a SQL query against the dataset and streams results using server-sent events (SSE). The request body contains a sql string (maximum 64 KB) and an optional params object for parameterized values. Parameterization is strongly recommended to prevent SQL injection and improve query plan caching. The SSE stream emits one JSON event per row produced by the query, a progress event every 5 seconds for long-running queries, and a final statistics event containing the row count, bytes scanned, execution duration, and cache hit indicator. The stream closes after the statistics event or on error. Queries are subject to a maximum execution time of 10 minutes; queries that exceed this threshold are terminated and the client receives an error event with the reason execution_timeout. GET /jobs For queries expected to exceed 10 minutes, use the asynchronous jobs endpoint. POST /jobs accepts the same request body as POST /query. The response returns a job_id and an estimated duration. The job runs in the background; results are written to a temporary S3 location accessible via GET /jobs/{job_id}/results. Jobs expire 24 hours after completion. Filtering and projection: To avoid transferring unnecessary columns, include a select array of column names in the query request. Server-side projection reduces response size and latency significantly for wide tables. Administration Endpoints User management: POST /users creates a user in the tenant with a specified role. The request body includes email, role, and an optional display_name. A welcome email with a credential setup link is sent automatically. PATCH /users/{user_id} updates a user's role or active status. DELETE /users/{user_id} deactivates the user and revokes all active sessions; the user record is retained for audit purposes. Role management: GET /roles returns the built-in roles

Thirty-day check-in: Focus on orientation completeness, understanding of role and team, initial observations, and any blockers the employee has encountered. Sixty-day check-in: Focus on early contribution, progress on initial goals, quality of cross-functional relationships, and development of role-specific skills. The manager should assess whether the employee is ramping at the expected pace and provide specific feedback on early work. Ninety-day check-in: Focus on independent contribution, alignment on annual goals, and identification of any development needs or growth opportunities. The ninety-day check-in is the final structured onboarding milestone. By this point, the employee should be operating with minimal supervision within their defined scope. Employees who are not on track at the ninety-day mark should be placed on an informal development plan to address specific gaps before the six-month mark. If performance concerns persist at six months, a formal PIP may be initiated. Manager Responsibilities Managers are responsible for the quality and fairness of the performance process for their direct reports. Specific responsibilities include: providing ongoing, real-time feedback throughout the year rather than saving all feedback for the annual review; conducting the mid-year and annual reviews on schedule; writing specific, evidence-based assessments; avoiding bias in ratings, including recency bias, similarity bias, and leniency or severity bias; delivering difficult feedback directly and constructively; and following through on development commitments made during review conversations. People Operations conducts manager calibration training each year before the review cycle opens. Participation is required for all people managers. Managers who consistently receive peer or employee feedback indicating unfair or unclear performance management practices are subject to coaching or additional accountability measures. Employees who believe their review was inaccurate, biased, or conducted improperly may raise a concern with People Operations. People Operations will review the manager's documentation and calibration record and determine whether any corrective action is warranted.

not acceptable. SQL and query injection: Parameterized queries or prepared statements must be used for all database queries. String concatenation for query construction is prohibited. Output encoding: Data rendered in HTML must be encoded to prevent cross-site scripting. Use framework-provided escaping functions. Do not construct HTML strings by concatenation. Cryptography: Do not implement custom cryptographic algorithms. Use vetted libraries. AES-256 is required for symmetric encryption. RSA-2048 or ECDSA with P-256 is the minimum for asymmetric encryption. SHA-256 or stronger is required for hashing. MD5 and SHA-1 are prohibited in security contexts. Secrets in code: No credentials, API keys, tokens, passwords, or private keys may appear in source code, configuration files, or commit messages. Error handling: Error messages returned to clients must not include stack traces, internal system paths, database error messages, or other internal details that would assist an attacker. Dependency management: New dependencies must be reviewed for known vulnerabilities before being added. Use the approved vulnerability scanning tool to check before adding a new library. Dependencies must be pinned to specific versions. License compatibility with Apex Systems' commercial use must be verified. OWASP Top Ten Awareness Engineers are expected to understand and mitigate the OWASP Top Ten vulnerability categories in their work: Broken Access Control: Ensure that authorization logic correctly enforces role-based and object-level access. Test that users cannot access resources or perform actions outside their permitted scope. Cryptographic Failures: Encrypt sensitive data at rest and in transit. Use TLS 1.2 or higher for all network communications. Do not store passwords in plaintext; use bcrypt or Argon2 with appropriate cost parameters. Injection: Apply parameterized queries, input validation, and output encoding to prevent SQL, command, LDAP, and other injection attacks. Insecure Design: Threat model new features before implementation. Design systems to fail safely and to enforce security boundaries at the architecture level. Security Misconfiguration: Harden deployments by disabling unnecessary features, removing default credentials, and enforcing secure defaults. Cloud storage buckets must not be publicly accessible unless explicitly required and approved. Infrastructure configuration is reviewed in code review. Vulnerable and Outdated Components: Keep dependencies up to date. Critical and high-severity CVEs in dependencies must be remediated within fourteen days of disclosure. Medium-severity CVEs must be remediated within sixty days. Identification and Authentication Failures: Implement rate limiting on authentication endpoints. Enforce MFA. Use secure session management practices including server-side session invalidation on logout. Software and Data Integrity Failures: Validate integrity of software artifacts in the build pipeline. Use code signing for published artifacts. Verify integrity of third-party data before processing. Security Logging and Monitoring Failures: Log authentication events, access to sensitive data, administrative actions, and security-relevant errors. Logs must be immutable and retained for at least one year. Security event logs are forwarded to the SIEM. Server-Side Request Forgery: Validate and restrict URLs in server-side requests. Do not allow user-controlled input to directly specify the destination of server-side HTTP requests without validation against an allowlist. Vulnerability Disclosure Apex Systems operates a responsible disclosure program. External security researchers who discover vulnerabilities in Apex Systems products may report them through the dedicated security disclosure email address

Vulnerability Disclosure Apex Systems operates a responsible disclosure program. External security researchers who discover vulnerabilities in Apex Systems products may report them through the dedicated security disclosure email address published on the company website. Reports are acknowledged within two business days. Internally discovered vulnerabilities are reported to the Security Engineering team through the security findings tracker. Engineers who discover a potential vulnerability in any production system, internal tool, or third-party component must report it to the Security Engineering team within twenty-four hours. Do not attempt to exploit a vulnerability beyond what is needed to confirm its existence. The Security Engineering team triages reported vulnerabilities and assigns a CVSS score and remediation priority. Critical vulnerabilities (CVSS 9.0 and above) require immediate escalation to the CTO and must have a remediation plan within twenty-four hours. High vulnerabilities (CVSS 7.0 to 8.9) must have a remediation plan within seven days. Medium vulnerabilities must be remediated within thirty days. Remediation is tracked in the security findings tracker. Penetration Testing Apex Systems conducts penetration testing at least once per year. The annual penetration test covers the customer-facing application, the API layer, the authentication and authorization system, and the cloud infrastructure. The test is conducted by an independent third-party security firm selected through the procurement process. In addition to the annual external test, internal red team exercises are conducted by the Security Engineering team on a quarterly basis, targeting specific high-risk components or recently launched features. Engineers may be engaged by the Security Engineering team to support red team exercises. All penetration test findings are reviewed by the CTO and the engineering leadership team. Critical and high findings must be remediated before the next production release cycle. All findings are tracked in the security findings tracker with named owners and target remediation dates. Security Incident Reporting Any actual or suspected security incident — including unauthorized access, data exposure, credential compromise, malware, phishing attempts that captured credentials, or suspicious system behavior — must be reported to the Security Engineering team immediately. Do not investigate security incidents independently. Preserve evidence by not modifying or deleting affected systems or logs before reporting. For confirmed security incidents, the incident response process (see the Incident Response Runbook) applies with Security Engineering leading the technical response. Incidents involving customer data require notification to the General Counsel within one hour of confirmation. Regulatory notification obligations are assessed by the Legal team. New engineers must complete security training within their first thirty days. Completion is tracked in the learning management system. Engineers who work on authentication, cryptography, or data privacy systems are required to complete role-specific security training in addition to the general annual training.
